From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

German Nazi paramilitary organisation (1925–1945)

"SS" and "German SS" redirect here. For the German letter 'ss', see [ß](/wiki/%C3%9F "ß"). For other uses, see [SS (disambiguation)](/wiki/SS_(disambiguation) "SS (disambiguation)").

*Schutzstaffel*

The ***Schutzstaffel*** (German: [[ˈʃʊtsˌʃtafl̩]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Schutzstaffel2.ogg "File:De-Schutzstaffel2.ogg"); lit. 'Protection Squadron'; **SS**; also stylised with [SS runes](/wiki/SS_runes "SS runes") as ***ᛋᛋ***) was a major [paramilitary](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary") organisation under [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") and the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), and later throughout [German-occupied Europe](/wiki/German-occupied_Europe "German-occupied Europe") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

It began with a small guard unit known as the *Saal-Schutz* ("Hall Security") made up of party volunteers to provide security for party meetings in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"). In 1925, [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") joined the unit, which had by then been reformed and given its final name. Under his direction (1929–1945) it grew from a small paramilitary formation during the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") to one of the most powerful organisations in Nazi Germany. From the time of the Nazi Party's rise to power until the regime's collapse in 1945, the SS was the foremost agency of security, [mass surveillance](/wiki/Mass_surveillance "Mass surveillance"), and [state terrorism](/wiki/State_terrorism "State terrorism") within Germany and German-occupied Europe.

The two main constituent groups were the *[Allgemeine SS](/wiki/Allgemeine_SS "Allgemeine SS")* (General SS) and *[Waffen-SS](/wiki/Waffen-SS "Waffen-SS")* (Armed SS). The *Allgemeine SS* was responsible for enforcing the [racial policy of Nazi Germany](/wiki/Racial_policy_of_Nazi_Germany "Racial policy of Nazi Germany") and general policing, whereas the *Waffen-SS* consisted of the combat units of the SS, with a sworn allegiance to Hitler. A third component of the SS, the *[SS-Totenkopfverbände](/wiki/SS-Totenkopfverb%C3%A4nde "SS-Totenkopfverbände")* (SS-TV; "[Death's Head](/wiki/Totenkopf "Totenkopf") Units"[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2009137-2)), ran the [concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps") and [extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp"). Additional subdivisions of the SS included the [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo") and the *[Sicherheitsdienst](/wiki/Sicherheitsdienst "Sicherheitsdienst")* (SD) organisations. They were tasked with detecting actual or potential enemies of Nazi Germany, neutralizing [the opposition](/wiki/German_resistance_to_Nazism "German resistance to Nazism"), policing the German people for their commitment to [Nazi ideology](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") and providing domestic and foreign intelligence.

The SS was the organisation most responsible for the genocidal murder of [an estimated 6 million Jews and millions of other victims](/wiki/Holocaust_victims "Holocaust victims") during [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008318-3) Members of all of its branches committed [war crimes](/wiki/War_crime "War crime") and [crimes against humanity](/wiki/Crimes_against_humanity "Crimes against humanity") during World War II (1939–1945). The SS was also involved in commercial enterprises and [exploited concentration camp inmates as slave labour](/wiki/Forced_labour_under_German_rule_during_World_War_II "Forced labour under German rule during World War II"). After Nazi Germany's defeat, the SS and the Nazi Party were judged by the [International Military Tribunal](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials") at Nuremberg to be criminal organisations. [Ernst Kaltenbrunner](/wiki/Ernst_Kaltenbrunner "Ernst Kaltenbrunner"), the highest-ranking surviving SS main department chief, was found guilty of crimes against humanity at the Nuremberg trials and hanged in 1946.

## Origins

### Forerunner of the SS

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Bundesarchiv_Bild_119-1486%2C_Hitler-Putsch%2C_M%C3%BCnchen%2C_Marienplatz.jpg/500px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_119-1486%2C_Hitler-Putsch%2C_M%C3%BCnchen%2C_Marienplatz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_119-1486,_Hitler-Putsch,_M%C3%BCnchen,_Marienplatz.jpg)

[Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") supporters and stormtroopers in Munich during the [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch"), 1923

By 1923, the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") led by [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") had created a small volunteer guard unit known as the *Saal-Schutz* (Hall Security) to provide security at their meetings in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2003228-4)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichaelDoerr2002356-5) The same year, Hitler ordered the formation of a small bodyguard unit dedicated to his personal service. He wished it to be separate from the "suspect mass" of the party, including the paramilitary *[Sturmabteilung](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung")* ("Storm Battalion"; SA), which he did not trust.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab200914,_16-6) The new formation was designated the *Stabswache* (Staff Guard).[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab200914-7) Originally the unit was composed of eight men, commanded by [Julius Schreck](/wiki/Julius_Schreck "Julius Schreck") and [Joseph Berchtold](/wiki/Joseph_Berchtold "Joseph Berchtold"), and was modelled after the [Erhardt Naval Brigade](/wiki/Marinebrigade_Ehrhardt "Marinebrigade Ehrhardt"), a *[Freikorps](/wiki/Freikorps "Freikorps")* of the time. The unit was renamed [*Stoßtrupp*](/wiki/Sto%C3%9Ftrupp-Hitler "Stoßtrupp-Hitler") (Shock Troops) in May 1923.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201016-8)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab200916-9)

The *Stoßtrupp* was abolished after the failed 1923 [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch"), an attempt by the Nazi Party to seize power in Munich.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHein201510-10) In 1925, Hitler ordered Schreck to organise a new bodyguard unit, the *Schutzkommando* (Protection Command).[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201026-1) It was tasked with providing personal protection for Hitler at party functions and events. That same year, the *Schutzkommando* was expanded to a national organisation and renamed successively the *Sturmstaffel* (Storm Squadron), and finally the *Schutzstaffel* (Protection Squad; SS).[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201026–29-11) Officially, the SS marked its foundation on 9 November 1925 (the second anniversary of the Beer Hall Putsch).[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoehl200434-12) The new SS protected party leaders throughout Germany. Hitler's personal SS protection unit was later enlarged to include combat units.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECookBender199417,_19-13)

### Early commanders

Schreck, a founding member of the SA and a close confidant of Hitler, became the first SS chief in March 1925.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaqueurBaumel2001604-14) On 15 April 1926, Joseph Berchtold succeeded him as chief of the SS. Berchtold changed the title of the office to *[Reichsführer-SS](/wiki/Reichsf%C3%BChrer-SS "Reichsführer-SS")* (Reich Leader-SS).[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201030-15) Berchtold was considered more dynamic than his predecessor but became increasingly frustrated by the authority the SA had over the SS.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201032-16) This led to him transferring leadership of the SS to his deputy, [Erhard Heiden](/wiki/Erhard_Heiden "Erhard Heiden"), on 1 March 1927.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHein201512-17) Under Heiden's leadership, a stricter code of discipline was enforced than would have been tolerated in the SA.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201032-16)

Between 1925 and 1929, the SS was considered to be a small *Gruppe* (battalion) of the SA.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201045–46-18) Except in the Munich area, the SS was unable to maintain any momentum in its membership numbers, which declined from 1,000 to 280 as the SA continued its rapid growth.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201032–33-19) As Heiden attempted to keep the SS from dissolving, [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") became his deputy in September 1927. Himmler displayed better organisational abilities than Heiden.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201045–46-18) The SS established [a number](/wiki/List_of_Gauleiters "List of Gauleiters") of [*Gaue*](/wiki/Gau_(territory) "Gau (territory)") (regions or provinces). The SS-Gaue consisted of *SS-Gau Berlin*, *SS-Gau Berlin Brandenburg*, *SS-Gau Franken*, *SS-Gau Niederbayern*, *SS-Gau Rheinland-Süd*, and *SS-Gau Sachsen*.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz20121–2-20)

### Himmler appointed

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1969-054-53A%2C_N%C3%BCrnberg%2C_Reichsparteitag.jpg/500px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1969-054-53A%2C_N%C3%BCrnberg%2C_Reichsparteitag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1969-054-53A,_N%C3%BCrnberg,_Reichsparteitag.jpg)

[Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") (with glasses, to the left of [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")) was an early supporter of the Nazi Party.

With Hitler's approval, Himmler assumed the position of *Reichsführer-SS* in January 1929.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab200918-21)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201047-22) There are differing accounts of the reason for Heiden's dismissal from his position as head of the SS. The party announced that it was for "family reasons".[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012113-23) Under Himmler, the SS expanded and gained a larger foothold. He considered the SS an elite, ideologically driven National Socialist organisation, a "conflation of [Teutonic knights](/wiki/Teutonic_Order "Teutonic Order"), the [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits"), and [Japanese Samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai")".[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurleighWippermann1991272–273-24) His ultimate aim was to turn the SS into the most powerful organisation in Germany and the most influential branch of the party.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201045–47,_300–305-25) He expanded the SS to 3,000 members in his first year as its leader.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurleighWippermann1991272–273-24)

In 1929, the *[SS-Hauptamt](/wiki/SS_Main_Office "SS Main Office")* (main SS office) was expanded and reorganised into five main offices dealing with general administration, personnel, finance, security, and race matters. At the same time, the SS-Gaue were divided into three *SS-Oberführerbereiche* areas, namely the *SS-Oberführerbereich Ost*, *SS-Oberführerbereich West*, and *SS-Oberführerbereich Süd*.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz20122–3-26) The lower levels of the SS remained largely unchanged. Although officially still considered a sub-organisation of the SA and answerable to the *[Stabschef](/wiki/Stabschef "Stabschef")* (SA Chief of Staff), it was also during this time that Himmler began to establish the independence of the SS from the SA.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008308–314-27) The SS grew in size and power due to its exclusive loyalty to Hitler, as opposed to the SA, which was seen as semi-independent and a threat to Hitler's hegemony over the party, mainly because they demanded a "second revolution" beyond the one that brought the Nazi Party to power.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaranowski2010196–197-28) By the end of 1933, the membership of the SS reached 209,000.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZentnerBedürftig1991901-29) Under Himmler's leadership, the SS continued to gather greater power as more and more state and party functions were assigned to its jurisdiction. Over time the SS became answerable only to Hitler, a development typical of the organisational structure of the entire Nazi regime, where legal norms were replaced by actions undertaken under the *[Führerprinzip](/wiki/F%C3%BChrerprinzip "Führerprinzip")* (leader principle), where Hitler's will was considered to be above the law.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZentnerBedürftig1991903-30)

In the latter half of 1934, Himmler oversaw the creation of *[SS-Junkerschule](/wiki/SS-Junker_Schools "SS-Junker Schools")*, institutions where SS officer candidates received leadership training, political and ideological indoctrination, and military instruction. The training stressed ruthlessness and toughness as part of the SS value system, which helped foster a sense of superiority among the men and taught them self-confidence.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaqueurBaumel2001606-31) The first schools were established at [Bad Tölz](/wiki/Bad_T%C3%B6lz "Bad Tölz") and [Braunschweig](/wiki/Braunschweig "Braunschweig"), with additional schools opening at [Klagenfurt](/wiki/Klagenfurt "Klagenfurt") and [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") during the war.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen2002112-32)

### Ideology

Main article: [Ideology of the SS](/wiki/Ideology_of_the_SS "Ideology of the SS")

The SS was regarded as the Nazi Party's elite unit.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöhne2001146,_147-33) In keeping with the [racial policy of Nazi Germany](/wiki/Racial_policy_of_Nazi_Germany "Racial policy of Nazi Germany"), in the early days all SS officer candidates had to provide proof of [Aryan ancestry](/wiki/Aryan_race#Nazism "Aryan race") back to 1750 and for other ranks to 1800.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg2002116-34) Once the war started and it became more difficult to confirm ancestry, the regulation was amended to proving only the candidate's grandparents were Aryan, as spelled out in the [Nuremberg Laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobsen199982,_93-35) Other requirements were complete obedience to the *Führer* and a commitment to the German people and nation.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201062–67-36) Himmler also tried to institute physical criteria based on appearance and height, but these requirements were only loosely enforced, and over half the SS men did not meet the criteria.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201063–65-37) Inducements such as higher salaries and larger homes were provided to members of the SS since they were expected to produce more children than the average German family as part of their commitment to Nazi Party doctrine.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELangerbein200319-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Gruft_der_Wewelsburg_%2810573265394%29_b.jpg/500px-Gruft_der_Wewelsburg_%2810573265394%29_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gruft_der_Wewelsburg_(10573265394)_b.jpg)

The crypt at [Wewelsburg](/wiki/Wewelsburg "Wewelsburg") was repurposed by Himmler as a place to memorialise dead SS members.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne2010115-39) Artwork commemorating the Holocaust hangs on the walls (2013).

Commitment to SS ideology was emphasised throughout the recruitment, membership process, and training.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöhne2001148–149-40) Members of the SS were indoctrinated in the racial policy of Nazi Germany and were taught that it was necessary to remove from Germany people deemed by that policy as inferior.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201065–66-41) [Esoteric](/wiki/Esoteric_Nazism "Esoteric Nazism") rituals and the awarding of regalia and insignia for milestones in the SS man's career suffused SS members even further with Nazi ideology.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöhne2001150–151-42) Members were expected to renounce their Christian faith, and Christmas was replaced with a [solstice celebration](/wiki/Christmas_in_Nazi_Germany "Christmas in Nazi Germany").[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne201093-43) Church weddings were replaced with SS *Eheweihen*, a pagan ceremony invented by Himmler.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne201094-44) These pseudo-religious rites and ceremonies often took place near SS-dedicated monuments or in special SS-designated places.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaqueurBaumel2001608-45) In 1933, Himmler bought [Wewelsburg](/wiki/Wewelsburg "Wewelsburg"), a castle in [Westphalia](/wiki/Westphalia "Westphalia"). He initially intended it to be used as an SS training centre, but its role came to include hosting SS dinners and neo-pagan rituals.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne2010111–113-46)

In 1936, Himmler wrote in the pamphlet "The SS as an Anti-Bolshevist Fighting Organisation":

> We shall take care that never again in Germany, the heart of Europe, will the Jewish-Bolshevik revolution of subhumans be able to be kindled either from within or through emissaries from without.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHimmler1936-47)

The SS ideology included the application of brutality and terror as a solution to military and political issues.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELangerbein200321-48) The SS stressed total loyalty and obedience to orders unto death. Hitler used this as a powerful tool to further his aims and those of the Nazi Party. The SS was entrusted with the commission of war crimes such as the murder of Jewish civilians. Himmler once wrote that an SS man "hesitates not for a single instant, but executes unquestioningly..." any *Führer-Befehl* (*Führer* order).[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHimmler1936134-49) Their official motto was *"[Meine Ehre heißt Treue](/wiki/Meine_Ehre_hei%C3%9Ft_Treue "Meine Ehre heißt Treue")"* (My Honour is Loyalty).[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201260–61-50)

As part of its race-centric functions during World War II, the SS oversaw the isolation and displacement of [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") from the populations of the conquered territories, seizing their assets and deporting them to concentration camps and [ghettos](/wiki/Jewish_ghettos_established_by_Nazi_Germany "Jewish ghettos established by Nazi Germany"), where they were used as slave labour or immediately murdered.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobsen199982,_93-35) Chosen to implement the [Final Solution](/wiki/Final_Solution "Final Solution") ordered by Hitler, the SS were the main group responsible for the institutional murder and [democide](/wiki/Democide "Democide") of more than 20 million people during the Holocaust, including approximately 5.2 million[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERummel199212–13-51) to 6 million[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008318-3) Jews and 10.5 million [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERummel199212–13-51) A significant number of victims were members of other racial or ethnic groups such as the 258,000 [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERummel199212–13-51) The SS was involved in murdering people viewed as threats to [race hygiene](/wiki/Nazi_eugenics "Nazi eugenics") or Nazi ideology, including the mentally or physically disabled, homosexuals, and political dissidents. Members of trade unions and those perceived to be affiliated with groups that opposed the regime (religious, political, social, and otherwise), or those whose views were contradictory to the goals of the Nazi Party government, were rounded up in large numbers; these included clergy of all faiths, [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), [Freemasons](/wiki/Freemasonry "Freemasonry"), [Communists](/wiki/Communism "Communism"), and [Rotary Club](/wiki/Rotary_International "Rotary International") members.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERummel199212-52) According to the judgements rendered at the [Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials"), as well as many war crimes investigations and trials conducted since then, the SS was responsible for the majority of Nazi war crimes. In particular, it was the primary organisation that carried out the Holocaust.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Military_Tribunal1946-53)

## Pre-war Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/HimmlerAndHeydrich_1938.jpeg/250px-HimmlerAndHeydrich_1938.jpeg)](/wiki/File:HimmlerAndHeydrich_1938.jpeg)

[Reinhard Heydrich](/wiki/Reinhard_Heydrich "Reinhard Heydrich") (right) was Himmler's protégé and a leading SS figure until his assassination in 1942.

After Hitler and the Nazi Party came to power on 30 January 1933, the SS was considered a state organisation and a branch of the government.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams200177-54) Law enforcement gradually became the purview of the SS, and many SS organisations became *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* government agencies.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuchheim1968157-55)

The SS established a [police state](/wiki/Police_state "Police state") within Nazi Germany, using the secret state police and security forces under Himmler's control to suppress resistance to Hitler.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHein201566–71-56) In his role as [Minister President of Prussia](/wiki/Minister_President_of_Prussia "Minister President of Prussia"), [Hermann Göring](/wiki/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Hermann Göring") had in 1933 created a Prussian [secret police](/wiki/Secret_police "Secret police") force, the *Geheime Staatspolizei* or [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo"), and appointed [Rudolf Diels](/wiki/Rudolf_Diels "Rudolf Diels") as its head. Concerned that Diels was not ruthless enough to use the Gestapo effectively to counteract the power of the SA, Göring handed over its control to Himmler on 20 April 1934.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans200554-57) Also on that date, in a departure from long-standing German practice that law enforcement was a state and local matter, Hitler appointed Himmler chief of all German police outside Prussia. Himmler named his deputy and protégé [Reinhard Heydrich](/wiki/Reinhard_Heydrich "Reinhard Heydrich") chief of the Gestapo on 22 April 1934. Heydrich also continued as head of the *[Sicherheitsdienst](/wiki/Sicherheitsdienst "Sicherheitsdienst")* (SD; security service).[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams200161-58)

The Gestapo's transfer to Himmler was a prelude to the [Night of the Long Knives](/wiki/Night_of_the_Long_Knives "Night of the Long Knives"), in which most of the SA leadership were arrested and subsequently executed.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHildebrand198413–14-59) The SS and Gestapo carried out most of the murders. On 20 July 1934, Hitler detached the SS from the SA, which was no longer an influential force after the purge. The SS became an elite corps of the Nazi Party, answerable only to Hitler. Himmler's title of *Reichsführer-SS* now became his actual rank – and the highest rank in the SS, equivalent to the rank of [field marshal](/wiki/Generalfeldmarschall "Generalfeldmarschall") in the army (his previous rank was *[Obergruppenführer](/wiki/Obergruppenf%C3%BChrer "Obergruppenführer")*).[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008313,_316-60) As Himmler's position and authority grew, so in effect did his rank.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab20099,_17,_26–27,_30,_46–47-61)

On 17 June 1936, all police forces throughout Germany were united under the purview of Himmler and the SS.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuchheim1968157-55) Himmler and Heydrich thus became two of the most powerful men in the country's administration.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReitlinger198990-62) Police and intelligence forces brought under their administrative control included the SD, Gestapo, *[Kriminalpolizei](/wiki/Kriminalpolizei "Kriminalpolizei")* (Kripo; criminal investigative police), and *[Ordnungspolizei](/wiki/Ordnungspolizei "Ordnungspolizei")* (Orpo; regular uniformed police).[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDearFoot1995814–815-63) In his capacity as police chief, Himmler was nominally subordinate to Interior Minister [Wilhelm Frick](/wiki/Wilhelm_Frick "Wilhelm Frick"). In practice, since the SS answered only to Hitler, the de facto merger of the SS and the police made the police independent of Frick's control.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams200177-54)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012204-64) In September 1939, the security and police agencies, including the *[Sicherheitspolizei](/wiki/Sicherheitspolizei "Sicherheitspolizei")* (SiPo; security police) and SD (but not the Orpo), were consolidated into the [Reich Security Main Office](/wiki/Reich_Security_Main_Office "Reich Security Main Office") (RSHA), headed by Heydrich.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012470-65) This further increased the collective authority of the SS.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHein201570–71-66)

During *[Kristallnacht](/wiki/Kristallnacht "Kristallnacht")* (9–10 November 1938), SS security services clandestinely coordinated violence against Jews as the SS, Gestapo, SD, Kripo, SiPo, and regular police did what they could to ensure that while Jewish synagogues and community centres were destroyed, Jewish-owned businesses and housing remained intact so that they could later be seized.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERead2005512–514-67) In the end, thousands of Jewish businesses, homes, and graveyards were vandalised and looted, particularly by members of the SA. Some 500 to 1,000 synagogues were destroyed, mostly by arson.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005584-68) On 11 November, Heydrich reported a death toll of 36 people, but later assessments put the number of deaths at up to two thousand.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERead2005515-69)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005590-70) On Hitler's orders, around 30,000 Jewish men were arrested and sent to concentration camps by 16 November.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005591-71) As many as 2,500 of these people died in the following months.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERead2005515-69) It was at this point that the SS state began in earnest its campaign of terror against political and religious opponents, who they imprisoned without trial or judicial oversight for the sake of "security, re-education, or prevention".[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHildebrand198461–62-72)[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201085-73)

In September 1939, the authority of the SS expanded further when the senior SS officer in each military district also became its chief of police.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHildebrand198461-74) Most of these [SS and police leaders](/wiki/SS_and_police_leader "SS and police leader") held the rank of SS-*[Gruppenführer](/wiki/Gruppenf%C3%BChrer "Gruppenführer")* or above and answered directly to Himmler in all SS matters within their district. Their role was to police the population and oversee the activities of the SS men within their district.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoehl2004144,_148,_169,_176–177-75) By declaring an emergency, they could bypass the district administrative offices for the SS, SD, SiPo, *[SS-Totenkopfverbände](/wiki/SS-Totenkopfverb%C3%A4nde "SS-Totenkopfverbände")* (SS-TV; concentration camp guards), and Orpo, thereby gaining direct operational control of these groups.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2009165-76)

### Hitler's personal bodyguards

Main article: [Adolf Hitler's bodyguard](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler%27s_bodyguard "Adolf Hitler's bodyguard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H15390%2C_Berlin%2C_Kaserne_der_LSSAH%2C_Vergatterung.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H15390%2C_Berlin%2C_Kaserne_der_LSSAH%2C_Vergatterung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H15390,_Berlin,_Kaserne_der_LSSAH,_Vergatterung.jpg)

Troop inspection of the *[Leibstandarte SS Adolf Hitler](/wiki/1st_SS_Panzer_Division_Leibstandarte_SS_Adolf_Hitler "1st SS Panzer Division Leibstandarte SS Adolf Hitler")* in Berlin, 1938

As the SS grew in size and importance, so too did Hitler's personal protection forces.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpielvogel1992102–108-77) Three main SS groups were assigned to protect Hitler. In 1933, his larger personal bodyguard unit (previously the [1st SS-Standarte](/wiki/1st_SS-Standarte "1st SS-Standarte")) was called to Berlin to replace the Army Chancellery Guard, assigned to protect the [Chancellor of Germany](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECookBender19948,_9-78) [Sepp Dietrich](/wiki/Sepp_Dietrich "Sepp Dietrich") commanded the new unit, previously known as SS-Stabswache Berlin; the name was changed to *SS-Sonderkommando Berlin*. In November 1933, the name was changed to *Leibstandarte Adolf Hitler*. In April 1934, Himmler modified the name to *[Leibstandarte SS Adolf Hitler](/wiki/1st_SS_Panzer_Division_Leibstandarte_SS_Adolf_Hitler "1st SS Panzer Division Leibstandarte SS Adolf Hitler")* (LSSAH). The LSSAH guarded Hitler's private residences and offices, providing an outer ring of protection for the Führer and his visitors.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECookBender19949,_12,_17–19-79) LSSAH men manned sentry posts at the entrances to the old [Reich Chancellery](/wiki/Reich_Chancellery "Reich Chancellery") and the new Reich Chancellery.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2000157,_160,_165-80) The number of LSSAH guards was increased during special events.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2000166-81) At the [Berghof](/wiki/Berghof_(residence) "Berghof (residence)"), Hitler's residence in the [Obersalzberg](/wiki/Obersalzberg "Obersalzberg"), a large contingent of the LSSAH patrolled an extensive cordoned security zone.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2000181–186-82)

From 1941 forward, the *Leibstandarte* became four distinct entities, the *Waffen-SS* division (unconnected to Hitler's protection but a formation of the *Waffen-SS*), the Berlin Chancellory Guard, the SS security regiment assigned to the Obersalzberg, and a Munich-based bodyguard unit which protected Hitler when he visited his apartment and the [Brown House](/wiki/Brown_House,_Munich "Brown House, Munich") Nazi Party headquarters in Munich.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECookBender199417–19-83)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2000157,_160,_165,_166,_181–186-84) Although the unit was nominally under Himmler, Dietrich was the real commander and handled day-to-day administration.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECookBender199419,_33-85)

Two other SS units composed the inner ring of Hitler's protection. The *[SS-Begleitkommando des Führers](/wiki/SS-Begleitkommando_des_F%C3%BChrers "SS-Begleitkommando des Führers")* (Escort Command of the Führer), formed in February 1932, served as Hitler's protection escort while he was travelling. This unit consisted of eight men who served around the clock protecting Hitler in shifts.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann200032,_48,_57-86) Later the *SS-Begleitkommando* was expanded and became known as the *Führerbegleitkommando* (*Führer* Escort Command; FBK). It continued under separate command and remained responsible for Hitler's protection.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann200036–48-87) The *Führer Schutzkommando* (*Führer* Protection Command; FSK) was a protection unit founded by Himmler in March 1933.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJoachimsthaler1999288-88) Originally it was only charged with protecting Hitler while he was inside the borders of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"). In early 1934, they replaced the *SS-Begleitkommando* for Hitler's protection throughout Germany.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann200032-89) The FSK was renamed the *[Reichssicherheitsdienst](/wiki/Reichssicherheitsdienst "Reichssicherheitsdienst")* (Reich Security Service; RSD) in August 1935.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann200036-90) [Johann Rattenhuber](/wiki/Johann_Rattenhuber "Johann Rattenhuber"), chief of the RSD, for the most part, took his orders directly from Hitler.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann200036-90) The current FBK chief acted as his deputy. Wherever Hitler was in residence, members of the RSD and FBK would be present. RSD men patrolled the grounds and FBK men provided close security protection inside. The RSD and FBK worked together for security and personal protection during Hitler's trips and public events, but they operated as two groups and used separate vehicles.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFelton201432–33-91) By March 1938, both units wore the standard field grey uniform of the SS.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann200036,_48-92) The RSD uniform had the SD diamond on the lower left sleeve.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFelton201418-93)

### Concentration camps founded

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H26996%2C_KZ_Dachau%2C_Verbrennungsofen.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H26996%2C_KZ_Dachau%2C_Verbrennungsofen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H26996,_KZ_Dachau,_Verbrennungsofen.jpg)

Crematorium at [Dachau concentration camp](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp"), May 1945 (photo taken after liberation)

The SS was closely associated with Nazi Germany's concentration camp system. On 26 June 1933, Himmler appointed SS-*[Oberführer](/wiki/Oberf%C3%BChrer "Oberführer")* [Theodor Eicke](/wiki/Theodor_Eicke "Theodor Eicke") as [commandant](/wiki/Commandant "Commandant") of [Dachau concentration camp](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp"), one of the first Nazi concentration camps.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPadfield2001128–129-94) It was created to consolidate the many small camps that had been set up by various police agencies and the Nazi Party to house political prisoners.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201095-95) The organisational structure Eicke instituted at Dachau stood as the model for all later concentration camps.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans200585-96) After 1934, Eicke was named commander of the *SS-Totenkopfverbände* (SS-TV), the SS formation responsible for running the concentration camps under the authority of the SS and Himmler.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilberg1985222-97) Known as the "Death's Head Units", the SS-TV was first organised as several battalions, each based at one of Germany's major concentration camps. Leadership at the camps was divided into five departments: commander and adjutant, political affairs division, protective custody, administration, and medical personnel.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHein201563-98) By 1935, Himmler secured Hitler's approval and the finances necessary to establish and operate additional camps.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachsmann201022-99) Six concentration camps[[a]](#cite_note-101) housing 21,400 inmates (mostly political prisoners) existed at the start of the war in September 1939.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2010108-102) By the end of the war, hundreds of camps of varying size and function had been created, holding nearly 715,000 people, most of whom were targeted by the regime because of their race.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008366–367-103)[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2010108–109-104) The concentration camp population rose in tandem with the defeats suffered by the Nazi regime; the worse the catastrophe seemed, the greater the fear of subversion, prompting the SS to intensify their repression and terror.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAyçoberry1999273-105)

## SS in World War II

By the outbreak of World War II, the SS had consolidated into its final form, which comprised three main organisations: the *Allgemeine SS*, *SS-Totenkopfverbände*, and the *Waffen-SS*, which was founded in 1934 as the *[SS-Verfügungstruppe](/wiki/SS-Verf%C3%BCgungstruppe "SS-Verfügungstruppe")* (SS-VT) and renamed in 1940.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein200223-106)[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004156-107) The *Waffen-SS* evolved into a second German army alongside the *[Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht")* and operated in tandem with them, especially with the *[Heer](/wiki/German_Army_(1935%E2%80%931945) "German Army (1935–1945)")* (German Army).[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002285–287-108) However, it never obtained total "independence of command", nor was it ever a "serious rival" to the German Army. Members were never able to join the ranks of the German High Command and it was dependent on the army for heavy weaponry and equipment.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein200218,_287-109) Although SS ranks generally had equivalents in the other services, the SS rank system did not copy the terms and ranks used by the *Wehrmacht*'s branches. Instead, it used the ranks established by the post-World War I *Freikorps* and the SA. This was primarily done to emphasise the SS as being independent of the *Wehrmacht*.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMollo19911–3-110)

### Invasion of Poland

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-380-0069-33%2C_Polen%2C_Verhaftung_von_Juden%2C_Transport.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-380-0069-33%2C_Polen%2C_Verhaftung_von_Juden%2C_Transport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-380-0069-33,_Polen,_Verhaftung_von_Juden,_Transport.jpg)

Polish Jews arrested by the *[Sicherheitsdienst](/wiki/Sicherheitsdienst "Sicherheitsdienst")* (SD) and police, September 1939

In the September 1939 [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland"), the LSSAH and SS-VT fought as separate mobile infantry regiments.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein200227-111) The LSSAH became notorious for torching villages without military justification.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButler200145-112) Members of the LSSAH committed war crimes in numerous towns, including the murder of 50 Polish Jews in [Błonie](/wiki/B%C5%82onie "Błonie") and the massacre of 200 civilians, including children, who were machine-gunned in [Złoczew](/wiki/Z%C5%82oczew "Złoczew"). Shootings also took place in [Bolesławiec](/wiki/Boles%C5%82awiec "Bolesławiec"), [Torzeniec](/wiki/Torzeniec "Torzeniec"), [Goworowo](/wiki/Goworowo,_P%C5%82o%C5%84sk_County "Goworowo, Płońsk County"), [Mława](/wiki/M%C5%82awa "Mława"), and [Włocławek](/wiki/W%C5%82oc%C5%82awek "Włocławek").[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossino2003114,_159–161-113) Some senior members of the *Wehrmacht* were not convinced the units were fully prepared for combat. Its units took unnecessary risks and had a higher casualty rate than the army.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004149-114) *[Generaloberst](/wiki/Generaloberst "Generaloberst")* [Fedor von Bock](/wiki/Fedor_von_Bock "Fedor von Bock") was quite critical; following an April 1940 visit of the *SS-Totenkopf* division, he found their battle training was "insufficient".[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHein201582-115) Hitler thought the criticism was typical of the army's "outmoded conception of chivalry."[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStone2011127-116) In its defence, the SS insisted that its armed formations had been hampered by having to fight piecemeal and were improperly equipped by the army.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004149-114)

After the invasion, Hitler entrusted the SS with extermination actions codenamed [Operation Tannenberg](/wiki/Operation_Tannenberg "Operation Tannenberg") and [AB-Aktion](/wiki/German_AB-Aktion_in_Poland "German AB-Aktion in Poland") to remove potential leaders who could form a resistance to German occupation. The murders were committed by *[Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen")* (task forces; deployment groups), assisted by local paramilitary groups. Men for the *Einsatzgruppen* units were drawn from the SS, the SD, and the police.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010144–145-117) Seven *Einsatzgruppen* were deployed in Poland, and four were particularly active in carrying out mass killings.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossino200390–92-118) Victims included [Polish nationalists](/wiki/Polish_nationalism "Polish nationalism"), Roman Catholic clergy, Jews, members of the nobility and [intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia "Intelligentsia"), as well as activists, scholars, teachers, and former officers. By the end of 1939, SS units aided by ethnic German auxiliaries had murdered approximately 50,000 Poles, including 7,000 Polish Jews, with broader estimates of Polish civilian deaths reaching up to 65,000 during this period.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans200814–15-119)[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004109–111-120)[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestermann2025-121) *Einsatzgruppe* [Woyrsch](/wiki/Udo_von_Woyrsch "Udo von Woyrsch") began large-scale shootings, especially of Jews, in towns such as [Tarnów](/wiki/Tarn%C3%B3w "Tarnów") and [Katowice](/wiki/Katowice "Katowice") and [Sosnowiec](/wiki/Sosnowiec "Sosnowiec") during the first weeks of September 1939.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowning200417–18-122)[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossino200390–92-118) When the army leadership registered complaints about the brutality being meted out by the *Einsatzgruppen*, Heydrich informed them that he was acting "in accordance with the special order of the *Führer*."[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2001246-123)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1968-034-19A%2C_Exekution_von_polnischen_Geiseln.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1968-034-19A%2C_Exekution_von_polnischen_Geiseln.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1968-034-19A,_Exekution_von_polnischen_Geiseln.jpg)

Murder of civilians by *[Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen")* in [Kórnik](/wiki/K%C3%B3rnik "Kórnik"), Poland, 1939

Satisfied with their performance in Poland, Hitler allowed further expansion of the armed SS formations but insisted new units remain under the operational control of the army.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReynolds19976,_7-124) While the *SS-Leibstandarte* remained an independent regiment functioning as Hitler's personal bodyguards, the other regiments—*SS-Deutschland*, *SS-Germania,* and *SS-Der Führer*—were combined to form the *[SS-Verfügungs-Division](/wiki/2nd_SS_Panzer_Division_Das_Reich "2nd SS Panzer Division Das Reich")*.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein200232-125)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004149-114) A second SS division, the *SS-Totenkopf*, was formed from SS-TV concentration camp guards, and a third, the [*SS-Polizei*](/wiki/4th_SS_Polizei_Panzergrenadier_Division "4th SS Polizei Panzergrenadier Division"), was created from police volunteers.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein200233–35-126)[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab200966-127) The SS gained control over its own recruitment, logistics, and supply systems for its armed formations at this time.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab200966-127) The SS, Gestapo, and SD were in charge of the provisional military administration in Poland until the appointment of [Hans Frank](/wiki/Hans_Frank "Hans Frank") as Governor-General on 26 October 1939.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHildebrand198450-128)[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2010229-129)

### Battle of France

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101III-Weill-059-04%2C_Metz%2C_Heinrich_Himmler_neben_Panzer.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101III-Weill-059-04%2C_Metz%2C_Heinrich_Himmler_neben_Panzer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101III-Weill-059-04,_Metz,_Heinrich_Himmler_neben_Panzer.jpg)

Himmler inspecting a [Sturmgeschütz III](/wiki/Sturmgesch%C3%BCtz_III "Sturmgeschütz III") of the [1st SS Panzer Division *"Leibstandarte SS Adolf Hitler"*](/wiki/1st_SS_Panzer_Division_Leibstandarte_SS_Adolf_Hitler "1st SS Panzer Division Leibstandarte SS Adolf Hitler") in [Metz](/wiki/Metz "Metz"), France, September 1940

On 10 May 1940, Hitler launched the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France"), a major offensive against France and the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries").[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHellwinkel20149-130) The SS supplied two of the 89 divisions employed.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReitlinger1989147-131) The LSSAH and elements of the SS-VT participated in the ground invasion [of the Netherlands](/wiki/German_invasion_of_the_Netherlands "German invasion of the Netherlands").[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein200261-132) Simultaneously, airborne troops were dropped to capture key Dutch airfields, bridges, and railways. In the five-day campaign, the LSSAH linked up with army units and airborne troops after several clashes with Dutch defenders.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein200261-132)

SS troops did not take part in the thrust through the [Ardennes](/wiki/Ardennes "Ardennes") and the river [Meuse](/wiki/Meuse "Meuse").[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein200261-132) Instead, the *SS-Totenkopf* was summoned from the army reserve to fight in support of *[Generalmajor](/wiki/Generalmajor "Generalmajor")* [Erwin Rommel](/wiki/Erwin_Rommel "Erwin Rommel")'s [7th Panzer Division](/wiki/7th_Panzer_Division_(Wehrmacht) "7th Panzer Division (Wehrmacht)") as they advanced toward the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel").[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButler200364-133) On 21 May, the British launched an armoured counterattack against the flanks of the 7th Panzer Division and *SS-Totenkopf*. The Germans then trapped the British and French troops in a huge pocket at [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk").[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning199959-134) On 27 May, 4 Company *SS-Totenkopf* perpetrated the [Le Paradis massacre](/wiki/Le_Paradis_massacre "Le Paradis massacre"), where 97 men of the 2nd Battalion, [Royal Norfolk Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Norfolk_Regiment "Royal Norfolk Regiment") were machine-gunned after surrendering, with survivors finished off with [bayonets](/wiki/Bayonet "Bayonet"). Two men survived.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESydnor197793-135) By 28 May the *SS-Leibstandarte* had taken [Wormhout](/wiki/Wormhout "Wormhout"), 10 miles (16 km) from Dunkirk. There, soldiers of the 2nd Battalion were responsible for the [Wormhoudt massacre](/wiki/Wormhoudt_massacre "Wormhoudt massacre"), where 81 British and French soldiers were murdered after they surrendered.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012251-136) According to historian Charles Sydnor, the "fanatical recklessness in the assault, suicidal defence against enemy attacks, and savage atrocities committed in the face of frustrated objectives" exhibited by the *SS-Totenkopf* division during the invasion were typical of the SS troops as a whole.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESydnor1977102-137)

At the close of the campaign, Hitler expressed his pleasure with the performance of the *SS-Leibstandarte*, telling them: "Henceforth it will be an honour for you, who bear my name, to lead every German attack."[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004143-138) The SS-VT was renamed the *Waffen-SS* in a speech made by Hitler in July 1940.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004156-107) Hitler then authorised the enlistment of "people perceived to be of related stock", as Himmler put it, to expand the ranks.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002150,_153-139) Danes, Dutch, Norwegians, Swedes, and Finns volunteered to fight in the *Waffen-SS* under the command of German officers.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoehl2004213–214-140) They were brought together to form the new division *[SS-Wiking](/wiki/5th_SS_Panzer_Division_Wiking "5th SS Panzer Division Wiking")*.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002150,_153-139) In January 1941, the *SS-Verfügungs* Division was renamed *SS-Reich* Division (Motorised), and was renamed as the [2nd SS Panzer Division *"Das Reich"*](/wiki/2nd_SS_Panzer_Division_Das_Reich "2nd SS Panzer Division Das Reich") when it was reorganised as a *[Panzergrenadier](/wiki/Panzergrenadier "Panzergrenadier")* division in 1942.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattson200277,_104-141)

### Campaign in the Balkans

In April 1941, the German Army [invaded Yugoslavia](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia") and [Greece](/wiki/German_invasion_of_Greece "German invasion of Greece"). The LSSAH and *Das Reich* were attached to separate army [Panzer corps](/wiki/Panzer_corps "Panzer corps"). [Fritz Klingenberg](/wiki/Fritz_Klingenberg "Fritz Klingenberg"), a company commander in the *Das Reich* division, led his men across Yugoslavia to the capital, [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade"), where a small group in the vanguard accepted the surrender of the city on 13 April. A few days later Yugoslavia surrendered.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004162,_163-142)[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012297-143) SS police units immediately began taking hostages and carrying out reprisals, a practice that became common. In some cases, they were joined by the *Wehrmacht*.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBessel2006110–111-144) Similar to Poland, the war policies of the Nazis in the Balkans resulted in brutal occupation and racist mass murder. Serbia became the second country (after [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia")) declared *[Judenfrei](/wiki/Judenfrei "Judenfrei")* (free of Jews).[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBessel2006110-145)

In Greece, the *Wehrmacht* and *Waffen-SS* encountered resistance from the [British Expeditionary Force](/wiki/British_Expeditionary_Force_(World_War_II) "British Expeditionary Force (World War II)") (BEF) and the [Greek Army](/wiki/Hellenic_Army "Hellenic Army").[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004163,_165-146) The fighting was intensified by the mountainous terrain, with its heavily defended narrow passes. The LSSAH was at the forefront of the German push.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004163–166-147) The BEF evacuated by sea to [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete"), but had to flee again in late May when the Germans arrived.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008155-148) Like Yugoslavia, the conquest of Greece brought its Jews into danger, as the Nazis immediately took a variety of measures against them.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBessel2006111-149) Initially confined in ghettos, most were transported to [Auschwitz concentration camp](/wiki/Auschwitz_concentration_camp "Auschwitz concentration camp") in March 1943, where they were murdered in the [gas chambers](/wiki/Gas_chamber "Gas chamber") on arrival. Of Greece's 80,000 Jews, only 20 per cent survived the war.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrusetta2012266-150)

## War in the east

On 22 June 1941, Hitler launched [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa"), the invasion of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGlantz20017–9-151) The expanding war and the need to control occupied territories provided the conditions for Himmler to further consolidate the police and military organs of the SS.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBracher1970409-152) Rapid acquisition of vast territories in the East placed considerable strain on the SS police organisations as they struggled to adjust to the changing security challenges.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood200664-153)

The 1st and 2nd SS Infantry Brigades, which had been formed from surplus concentration camp guards of the SS-TV, and the [SS Cavalry Brigade](/wiki/SS_Cavalry_Brigade "SS Cavalry Brigade") moved into the Soviet Union behind the advancing armies. At first, they fought [Soviet partisans](/wiki/Soviet_partisans "Soviet partisans"), but by the autumn of 1941, they left the anti-partisan role to other units and actively took part in the Holocaust. While assisting the *Einsatzgruppen*, they formed firing parties that participated in the liquidation of the Jewish population of the Soviet Union.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWindrowBurn19929-154)[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeerNaumann2000136-155)

On 31 July 1941, Göring gave Heydrich written authorisation to ensure the cooperation of administrative leaders of various government departments to undertake genocide of the Jews in territories under German control.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowning2004315-156) Heydrich was instrumental in carrying out these exterminations, as the Gestapo was ready to organise deportations in the West and his *Einsatzgruppen* were already conducting extensive murder operations in the East.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilberg1985164-157) On 20 January 1942, Heydrich chaired a meeting, called the [Wannsee Conference](/wiki/Wannsee_Conference "Wannsee Conference"), to discuss the implementation of the plan.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008696–697-158)

During battles in the Soviet Union in 1941 and 1942, the *Waffen-SS* suffered enormous casualties. The LSSAH and *Das Reich* lost over half their troops to illness and combat casualties.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004168-159) In need of recruits, Himmler began to accept soldiers who did not fit the original SS racial profile.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004171-160) In early 1942, *SS-Leibstandarte*, *SS-Totenkopf*, and *SS-Das Reich* were withdrawn to the West to refit and were converted to *Panzergrenadier* divisions.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReynolds19979-161) The SS-Panzer Corps returned to the Soviet Union in 1943 and participated in the [Third Battle of Kharkov](/wiki/Third_Battle_of_Kharkov "Third Battle of Kharkov") in February and March.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004173-162)

### The Holocaust

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Einsatzgruppen_murder_Jews_in_Ivanhorod%2C_Ukraine%2C_1942.jpg/330px-Einsatzgruppen_murder_Jews_in_Ivanhorod%2C_Ukraine%2C_1942.jpg)](/wiki/Ivanhorod_Einsatzgruppen_photograph,_1942 "Ivanhorod Einsatzgruppen photograph, 1942")

Murder of Jews by *[Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen")* in [Ivanhorod](/wiki/Ivanhorod "Ivanhorod"), Ukraine, 1942

The SS was built on a culture of violence, which was exhibited in its most extreme form by the mass murder of civilians and prisoners of war on the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)").[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFritz201169–70,_94–108-163) Augmented by personnel from the Kripo, Orpo (Order Police), and *Waffen-SS*,[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrausnik196877-164) the *Einsatzgruppen* reached a total strength of 3,000 men. *Einsatzgruppen* A, B, and C were attached to [Army Groups North](/wiki/Army_Group_North "Army Group North"), [Centre](/wiki/Army_Group_Centre "Army Group Centre"), and [South](/wiki/Army_Group_South "Army Group South"); *Einsatzgruppe* D was assigned to the [11th Army](/wiki/11th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "11th Army (Wehrmacht)"). The *Einsatzgruppe* for Special Purposes operated in eastern Poland starting in July 1941.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010185-165) Historian [Richard Rhodes](/wiki/Richard_Rhodes "Richard Rhodes") describes them as being "outside the bounds of morality"; they were "judge, jury and executioner all in one", with the authority to kill anyone at their discretion.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2003159–160-166) Following Operation Barbarossa, these *Einsatzgruppen* units, together with the *Waffen-SS* and Order Police as well as with assistance from the *Wehrmacht*, engaged in the mass murder of the Jewish population in occupied eastern Poland and the Soviet Union.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2003159–160-166)[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBessel2006118–119-167)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg2007163-168) The greatest extent of *Einsatzgruppen* action occurred in 1941 and 1942 in Ukraine and Russia.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaqueurBaumel2001164-169) Before the invasion there were five million registered Jews throughout the Soviet Union, with three million of those residing in the territories occupied by the Germans; by the time the war ended, over two million of these had been murdered.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBessel2006119-170)

The extermination activities of the *Einsatzgruppen* generally followed a standard procedure, with the *Einsatzgruppen* chief contacting the nearest *Wehrmacht* unit commander to inform him of the impending action; this was done so they could coordinate and control access to the execution grounds.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZentnerBedürftig1991227-171) Initially, the victims were shot, but this method proved impracticable for an operation of this scale.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008256–257-172) Also, after Himmler observed the shooting of 100 Jews at [Minsk](/wiki/Minsk "Minsk") in August 1941, he grew concerned about the impact such actions were having on the mental health of his SS men. He decided that alternate methods of murder should be found, which led to the introduction of [gas vans](/wiki/Gas_van "Gas van").[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012547-173)[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerwarth2011199-174) However, these were not popular with the men, as they regarded removing the dead bodies from the van and burying them to have been unpleasant. Prisoners or auxiliaries were often assigned to do this task so as to spare the SS men the trauma.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2003243-175)

### Anti-partisan operations

Further information: [Bandenbekämpfung](/wiki/Bandenbek%C3%A4mpfung "Bandenbekämpfung")

In response to the army's difficulties in dealing with Soviet partisans, Hitler decided in July 1942 to transfer anti-partisan operations to the police. This placed the matter under Himmler's purview.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood200670–71-176)[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012625-177) As Hitler had ordered on 8 July 1941 that all Jews were to be regarded as partisans, the term "anti-partisan operations" was used as a euphemism for the murder of Jews as well as actual combat against resistance elements.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010198-178)[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012626,_629-179) In July 1942 Himmler ordered that the term "partisan" should no longer be used; instead resisters to Nazi rule would be described as "bandits".[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012627-180)

Himmler set the SS and SD to work on developing additional anti-partisan tactics and launched a [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda_in_Nazi_Germany "Propaganda in Nazi Germany") campaign.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood200671–77-181) Sometime in June 1943, Himmler issued the *[Bandenbekämpfung](/wiki/Bandenbek%C3%A4mpfung "Bandenbekämpfung")* (bandit fighting) order, simultaneously announcing the existence of the *Bandenkampfverbände* (bandit fighting formations), with *SS-Obergruppenführer* [Erich von dem Bach-Zelewski](/wiki/Erich_von_dem_Bach-Zelewski "Erich von dem Bach-Zelewski") as its chief. Employing troops primarily from the SS police and *Waffen-SS*, the *Bandenkampfverbände* had four principal operational components: propaganda, centralised control and coordination of security operations, training of troops, and battle operations.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood2006121-182) Once the *Wehrmacht* had secured its territorial objectives, the *Bandenkampfverbände* first secured communication facilities, roads, railways, and waterways. Subsequently, they secured rural communities and economic installations such as factories and administrative buildings. Additionally, they protected agricultural and forestry resources. The SS oversaw the collection of the harvest, which was considered critical to strategic operations.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood2006152–154-183) Any Jews in the area were rounded up and killed. Communists and people of Asiatic descent were killed presumptively under the assumption that they were Soviet agents.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012628–629-184)

### Death camps

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/May_1944_-_Jews_from_Carpathian_Ruthenia_arrive_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau.jpg/330px-May_1944_-_Jews_from_Carpathian_Ruthenia_arrive_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau.jpg)](/wiki/File:May_1944_-_Jews_from_Carpathian_Ruthenia_arrive_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau.jpg)

Jews from [Carpathian Ruthenia](/wiki/Carpathian_Ruthenia "Carpathian Ruthenia") arriving at [Auschwitz concentration camp](/wiki/Auschwitz_concentration_camp "Auschwitz concentration camp"), 1944

After the start of the war, Himmler intensified the activity of the SS within Germany and in Nazi-occupied Europe. Increasing numbers of Jews and German citizens deemed politically suspect or social outsiders were arrested.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachsmann201027-185) As the Nazi regime became more oppressive, the concentration camp system grew in size and lethal operation, and grew in scope as the economic ambitions of the SS intensified.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachsmann201026–27-186)

Intensification of the killing operations took place in late 1941 when the SS began construction of stationary gassing facilities to replace the use of *Einsatzgruppen* for mass murders.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerwarth2011208-187)[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010279–280-188) Victims at these new [extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp") were killed with the use of carbon monoxide gas from automobile engines.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008283-189) During [Operation Reinhard](/wiki/Operation_Reinhard "Operation Reinhard"), run by officers from the *Totenkopfverbände*, who were sworn to secrecy, three extermination camps were built in occupied Poland: [Bełżec](/wiki/Belzec_extermination_camp "Belzec extermination camp") (operational by March 1942), [Sobibór](/wiki/Sobibor_extermination_camp "Sobibor extermination camp") (operational by May 1942), and [Treblinka](/wiki/Treblinka_extermination_camp "Treblinka extermination camp") (operational by July 1942),[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008283,_287,_290-190) with squads of [Trawniki men](/wiki/Trawniki_men "Trawniki men") (Eastern European collaborators) overseeing hundreds of *[Sonderkommando](/wiki/Sonderkommando "Sonderkommando")* prisoners,[[b]](#cite_note-191) who were forced to work in the gas chambers and crematoria before being murdered themselves.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2009141-192) On Himmler's orders, by early 1942 the concentration camp at Auschwitz was greatly expanded to include the addition of gas chambers, where victims were killed using the pesticide [Zyklon B](/wiki/Zyklon_B "Zyklon B").[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008295,_299–300-193)[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachsmann201029-194)

For administrative reasons, all concentration camp guards and administrative staff became full members of the *Waffen-SS* in 1942. The concentration camps were placed under the command of the *SS-Wirtschafts-Verwaltungshauptamt* ([SS Main Economic and Administrative Office](/wiki/SS_Main_Economic_and_Administrative_Office "SS Main Economic and Administrative Office"); WVHA) under [Oswald Pohl](/wiki/Oswald_Pohl "Oswald Pohl").[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012559-195) [Richard Glücks](/wiki/Richard_Gl%C3%BCcks "Richard Glücks") served as the [Inspector of Concentration Camps](/wiki/Concentration_Camps_Inspectorate "Concentration Camps Inspectorate"), which in 1942 became office "D" under the WVHA.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoehl2004182–183-196)[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012115-197) Exploitation and extermination became a balancing act as the military situation deteriorated. The labour needs of the war economy, especially for skilled workers, meant that some Jews escaped the genocide.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGruner2012174–175-198) On 30 October 1942, due to severe labour shortages in Germany, Himmler ordered that large numbers of able-bodied people in Nazi-occupied Soviet territories be taken prisoner and sent to Germany as [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour_under_German_rule_during_World_War_II "Forced labour under German rule during World War II").[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012629-199)

By 1944, the SS-TV had been organised into three divisions: staff of the concentration camps in Germany and Austria, in the occupied territories, and of the extermination camps in Poland. By 1944, it became standard practice to rotate SS members in and out of the camps, partly based on manpower needs, but also to provide easier assignments to wounded *Waffen-SS* members.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReitlinger1989265-200) This rotation of personnel meant that nearly the entire SS knew what was going on inside the concentration camps, making the entire organisation liable for war crimes and [crimes against humanity](/wiki/Crimes_against_humanity "Crimes against humanity").[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002258–263-201)

## Business empire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Bundesarchiv_Bild_192-269%2C_KZ_Mauthausen%2C_H%C3%A4ftlinge_im_Steinbruch.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_192-269%2C_KZ_Mauthausen%2C_H%C3%A4ftlinge_im_Steinbruch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_192-269,_KZ_Mauthausen,_H%C3%A4ftlinge_im_Steinbruch.jpg)

At [Mauthausen-Gusen concentration camp](/wiki/Mauthausen_concentration_camp "Mauthausen concentration camp"), inmates were forced to carry heavy granite blocks out of the quarry on the "Stairs of Death".

In 1934, Himmler founded the first SS business venture, *Nordland-Verlag*, a publishing house that released propaganda material and SS training manuals. Thereafter, he purchased [Allach Porcelain](/wiki/Allach_(porcelain) "Allach (porcelain)"), which then began to produce SS memorabilia.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012114-202) Because of the labour shortage and a desire for financial gain, the SS started exploiting concentration camp inmates as slave labour.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004119,_120-203) Most of the SS businesses lost money until Himmler placed them under the administration of Pohl's *Verwaltung und Wirtschaftshauptamt Hauptamt* (Administration and Business office; VuWHA) in 1939.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012115-197) Even then, most of the enterprises did not fare well, as SS men were not selected for their business experience, and the workers were starving.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMazower2008312–313-204) In July 1940 Pohl established the *[Deutsche Wirtschaftsbetriebe GmbH](/wiki/Deutsche_Wirtschaftsbetriebe "Deutsche Wirtschaftsbetriebe")* (German Businesses Ltd; DWB), an umbrella corporation under which he took over administration of all SS business concerns.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012485-205) Eventually, the SS founded nearly 200 holding companies for their businesses.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012482-206)

In May 1941 the VuWHA founded the *Deutsche Ausrüstungswerke* GmbH (German Equipment Works; DAW), which was created to integrate the SS business enterprises with the burgeoning concentration camp system.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen200295-207) Himmler subsequently established four major new concentration camps in 1941: Auschwitz, [Gross-Rosen](/wiki/Gross-Rosen_concentration_camp "Gross-Rosen concentration camp"), [Natzweiler-Struthof](/wiki/Natzweiler-Struthof_concentration_camp "Natzweiler-Struthof concentration camp"), and [Neuengamme](/wiki/Neuengamme_concentration_camp "Neuengamme concentration camp"). Each had at least one factory or quarry nearby where the inmates were forced to work.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012480–481-208) Himmler took a particular interest in providing labourers for [IG Farben](/wiki/IG_Farben "IG Farben"), which was constructing a synthetic rubber factory at [Auschwitz III–Monowitz](/wiki/Monowitz_concentration_camp "Monowitz concentration camp").[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012480-209) The plant was almost ready to commence production when it was overrun by Soviet troops in 1945.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher2005129-210) The life expectancy of inmates at Monowitz averaged about three months.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200556-211) This was typical of the camps, as inmates were underfed and lived under disastrously bad living conditions. Their workload was intentionally made impossibly high, under the policy of [extermination through labour](/wiki/Extermination_through_labour "Extermination through labour").[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010316-212)

In 1942, Himmler consolidated all of the offices for which Pohl was responsible into one, creating the SS Main Economic and Administrative Office (*Wirtschafts- und Verwaltungshauptamt*; WVHA).[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012559-195) The entire concentration camp system was placed under the authority of the WVHA.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoehl2004182–183-196) The SS owned Sudetenquell GmbH, a mineral water producer in the [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland"). By 1944, the SS had purchased 75 per cent of the mineral water producers in Germany and were intending to acquire a monopoly.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012484-213) Several concentration camps produced building materials such as stone, bricks, and cement for the SS-owned *[Deutsche Erd- und Steinwerke](/wiki/German_Earth_and_Stone_Works "German Earth and Stone Works")* (German Earth And Stone Works; DEST).[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012114–115-214) In the occupied Eastern territories, the SS acquired a monopoly in brick production by seizing all 300 extant brickworks.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012484-213) The DWB also founded the *Ost-Deutsche Baustoffwerke* (East German Building Supply Works; GmbH or ODBS) and *Deutsche Edelmöbel* GmbH (German Noble Furniture). These operated in factories the SS had confiscated from Jews and Poles.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen2002102-215)

The SS owned experimental farms, bakeries, meat packing plants, leather works, clothing and uniform factories, and small arms factories.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012115–116-216)[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012483-217) Under the direction of the WVHA, the SS sold camp labour to various factories at a rate of three to six *[Reichsmarks](/wiki/Reichsmark "Reichsmark")* per prisoner per day.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrei1993128-218) The SS confiscated and sold the property of concentration camp inmates, confiscated their investment portfolios and their cash, and profited from their dead bodies by [selling their hair to make felt](/wiki/August_Frank_memorandum "August Frank memorandum") and melting down their dental work to obtain gold from the fillings.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012116-219) The total value of assets looted from the victims of Operation Reinhard alone (not including Auschwitz) was listed by [Odilo Globocnik](/wiki/Odilo_Globocnik "Odilo Globocnik") as 178,745,960.59 Reichsmarks. Items seized included 2,909.68 kg (6,414.7 lb) of gold worth 843,802.75 RM, as well as 18,733.69 kg (41,300.7 lb) of silver, 1,514 kg (3,338 lb) of platinum, 249,771.50 American dollars, 130 diamond solitaires, 2,511.87 carats of brilliants, 13,458.62 carats of diamonds, and 114 kg of pearls.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Military_Tribunal1950-220) According to Nazi legislation, Jewish property belonged to the state, but many SS camp commandants and guards stole items such as diamonds or currency for personal gain or took seized foodstuffs and alcohol to sell on the black market.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaxter201467-221)

## Military reversals

On 5 July 1943, the Germans launched the [Battle of Kursk](/wiki/Battle_of_Kursk "Battle of Kursk"), an offensive designed to eliminate the [Kursk](/wiki/Kursk "Kursk") salient.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008486-222) Three SS armored divisions of the II SS Panzer Corps ([1. SS "LSSAH"](/wiki/1st_SS_Panzer_Division_Leibstandarte_SS_Adolf_Hitler "1st SS Panzer Division Leibstandarte SS Adolf Hitler"), [2. SS "Das Reich"](/wiki/2nd_SS_Panzer_Division_Das_Reich "2nd SS Panzer Division Das Reich") and [3. SS "Totenkopf](/wiki/3rd_SS_Panzer_Division_Totenkopf "3rd SS Panzer Division Totenkopf")") participated alongside Wehrmacht Panzer divisions.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForczyk201713–15-223)[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZetterlingFrankson2016102–103-224) Due to stiff Soviet resistance, Hitler halted the attack by the evening of 12 July. On 17 July he called off the operation and ordered a withdrawal.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008488–489-225) Thereafter, the Germans were forced onto the defensive as the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") began the liberation of Western Russia.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab200968,_70-226) The losses incurred by the *Waffen-SS* and the *Wehrmacht* during the Battle of Kursk occurred nearly simultaneously with the [Allied assault into Italy](/wiki/Italian_campaign_(World_War_II) "Italian campaign (World War II)"), opening a two-front war for Germany.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFritz2011350-227)

### Normandy landings

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-263-1580-05%2C_Atlantikwall%2C_Soldaten_der_Legion_%22Freies_Indien%22.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-263-1580-05%2C_Atlantikwall%2C_Soldaten_der_Legion_%22Freies_Indien%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-263-1580-05,_Atlantikwall,_Soldaten_der_Legion_%22Freies_Indien%22.jpg)

Troops of the [Indian Legion](/wiki/Indian_Legion "Indian Legion") of the *Waffen-SS* guarding the [Atlantic Wall](/wiki/Atlantic_Wall "Atlantic Wall") in [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux"), France, 21 March 1944

Alarmed by the raids on [St Nazaire](/wiki/St_Nazaire_Raid "St Nazaire Raid") and [Dieppe](/wiki/Dieppe_Raid "Dieppe Raid") in 1942, Hitler had ordered the construction of fortifications he called the [Atlantic Wall](/wiki/Atlantic_Wall "Atlantic Wall") all along the Atlantic coast, from Spain to Norway, to protect against an expected Allied invasion.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordZaloga200930-228) Concrete gun emplacements were constructed at strategic points along the coast, and wooden stakes, metal tripods, mines, and large anti-tank obstacles were placed on the beaches to delay the approach of landing craft and impede the movement of tanks.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordZaloga200954–56-229) In addition to several static infantry divisions, eleven panzer and *Panzergrenadier* divisions were deployed nearby.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitmarsh200912,_13-230)[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordZaloga200960,_63,_122,_275-231) Four of these formations were *Waffen-SS* divisions.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002219-232) In addition, the *SS-Das Reich* was located in [Southern France](/wiki/Southern_France "Southern France"), the LSSAH was in Belgium refitting after fighting in the Soviet Union, and the newly formed panzer division *[SS-Hitlerjugend](/wiki/12th_SS_Panzer_Division_Hitlerjugend "12th SS Panzer Division Hitlerjugend")*, consisting of 17- and 18-year-old [Hitler Youth](/wiki/Hitler_Youth "Hitler Youth") members supported by combat veterans and experienced [NCOs](/wiki/Non-commissioned_officer "Non-commissioned officer"), was stationed west of Paris.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013295-233) The creation of the *SS-Hitlerjugend* was a sign of Hitler's desperation for more troops, especially ones with unquestioning obedience.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERempel1989233-234)

The [Normandy landings](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings") took place beginning on 6 June 1944. The [21st Panzer Division](/wiki/21st_Panzer_Division_(Wehrmacht) "21st Panzer Division (Wehrmacht)") under *[Generalmajor](/wiki/Generalmajor "Generalmajor")* [Edgar Feuchtinger](/wiki/Edgar_Feuchtinger "Edgar Feuchtinger"), positioned south of [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen"), was the only panzer division close to the beaches. The division included 146 tanks and 50 [assault guns](/wiki/Assault_gun "Assault gun"), plus supporting infantry and artillery.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitmarsh200973-235) At 02:00, *[Generalleutnant](/wiki/Generalleutnant "Generalleutnant")* Wilhelm Richter, commander of the [716th Static Infantry Division](/wiki/716th_Static_Infantry_Division_(Wehrmacht) "716th Static Infantry Division (Wehrmacht)"), ordered the 21st Panzer Division into position to counter-attack. However, as the division was part of the armoured reserve, Feuchtinger was obliged to seek clearance from [OKW](/wiki/Oberkommando_der_Wehrmacht "Oberkommando der Wehrmacht") before he could commit his formation.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordZaloga2009230-236) Feuchtinger did not receive orders until nearly 09:00, but in the meantime, on his own initiative he put together a battle group (including tanks) to fight the British forces east of the [Orne](/wiki/River_Orne_(Normandy) "River Orne (Normandy)").[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilmot1997282-237) *SS-Hitlerjugend* began to deploy in the afternoon of 6 June, with its units undertaking defensive actions the following day. They also took part in the [Battle for Caen](/wiki/Battle_for_Caen "Battle for Caen") (June–August 1944).[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013297-238) On 7–8 and 17 June, members of the *SS-Hitlerjugend* shot and killed twenty Canadian prisoners of war in the [Ardenne Abbey massacre](/wiki/Ardenne_Abbey_massacre "Ardenne Abbey massacre").[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab200973-239)

The Allies continued to make progress in the liberation of France, and on 4 August Hitler ordered a counter-offensive ([Operation Lüttich](/wiki/Operation_L%C3%BCttich "Operation Lüttich")) from [Vire](/wiki/Vire "Vire") towards [Avranches](/wiki/Avranches "Avranches").[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilmot1997399–400-240) The operation included LSSAH, *Das Reich*, [2nd](/wiki/2nd_Panzer_Division_(Wehrmacht) "2nd Panzer Division (Wehrmacht)"), and [116th Panzer Divisions](/wiki/116th_Panzer_Division_(Wehrmacht) "116th Panzer Division (Wehrmacht)"), with support from infantry and elements of the [17th SS Panzergrenadier Division *"Götz von Berlichingen"*](/wiki/17th_SS_Panzergrenadier_Division_G%C3%B6tz_von_Berlichingen "17th SS Panzergrenadier Division Götz von Berlichingen") under [*SS-Oberstgruppenführer*](/wiki/SS-Oberst-Gruppenf%C3%BChrer "SS-Oberst-Gruppenführer") [Paul Hausser](/wiki/Paul_Hausser "Paul Hausser"). These forces were to mount an offensive near [Mortain](/wiki/Mortain "Mortain") and drive west through Avranches to the coast. The Allied forces were prepared for this offensive, and an air assault on the combined German units proved devastating.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002222–223-241) On 21 August, 50,000 German troops, including most of the LSSAH, were encircled by the Allies in the [Falaise Pocket](/wiki/Falaise_pocket "Falaise pocket").[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilmot1997420-242) Remnants of the LSSAH which escaped were withdrawn to Germany for refitting.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013197-243) [Paris was liberated](/wiki/Liberation_of_Paris "Liberation of Paris") on 25 August, and the last of the German forces withdrew over the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") by the end of August, ending the Normandy campaign.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer19601085–1086-244)

### Battle for Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-J28510%2C_Ardennenoffensive%2C_deutsche_Infanterie_geht_im_Wald_vor..jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-J28510%2C_Ardennenoffensive%2C_deutsche_Infanterie_geht_im_Wald_vor..jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-J28510,_Ardennenoffensive,_deutsche_Infanterie_geht_im_Wald_vor..jpg)

German infantry travelling on foot in the Ardennes, December 1944

*Waffen-SS* units that had survived the summer campaigns were withdrawn from the front line to refit. Two of them, the [9th SS](/wiki/9th_SS_Panzer_Division_Hohenstaufen "9th SS Panzer Division Hohenstaufen") and [10th SS Panzer Divisions](/wiki/10th_SS_Panzer_Division_Frundsberg "10th SS Panzer Division Frundsberg"), did so in the [Arnhem](/wiki/Arnhem "Arnhem") region of Holland in early September 1944. Coincidentally, on 17 September, the Allies launched in the same area [Operation Market Garden](/wiki/Operation_Market_Garden "Operation Market Garden"), a combined airborne and land operation designed to seize control of the lower [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine").[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinberg1994701-245) The 9th and 10th Panzers were among the units that repulsed the attack.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurrayMillett2001439–442-246)

In December 1944, Hitler launched the Ardennes Offensive, also known as the [Battle of the Bulge](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Bulge "Battle of the Bulge"), a significant counterattack against the western Allies through the Ardennes with the aim of reaching [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") while encircling the Allied armies in the area.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinberg1994765–766-247) The offensive began with an artillery barrage shortly before dawn on 16 December.
