Spearheading the attack were two panzer armies composed largely of *Waffen-SS* divisions.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurrayMillett2001465-248) The battlegroups found advancing through the forests and wooded hills of the Ardennes difficult in the winter weather, but they initially made good progress in the northern sector. They soon encountered strong resistance from the US [2nd](/wiki/2nd_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "2nd Infantry Division (United States)") and [99th Infantry Divisions](/wiki/99th_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "99th Infantry Division (United States)"). By 23 December, the weather improved enough for Allied air forces to attack the German forces and their supply columns, causing fuel shortages. In increasingly difficult conditions, the German advance slowed and was stopped.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinberg1994767–769-249) Hitler's failed offensive cost 700 tanks and most of their remaining mobile forces in the west,[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinberg1994769-250) as well as most of their irreplaceable reserves of manpower and materiel.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002232-251)

During the battle, SS-*[Obersturmbannführer](/wiki/Obersturmbannf%C3%BChrer "Obersturmbannführer")* [Joachim Peiper](/wiki/Joachim_Peiper "Joachim Peiper") left a path of destruction, which included *Waffen-SS* soldiers under his command murdering American [POWs](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war "Prisoner of war") and unarmed Belgian civilians in the [Malmedy massacre](/wiki/Malmedy_massacre "Malmedy massacre").[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurrayMillett2001468-252) Captured SS soldiers who were part of *Kampfgruppe Peiper* were tried during the [Malmedy massacre trial](/wiki/Malmedy_massacre_trial "Malmedy massacre trial") following the war for this massacre and several others in the area. Many of the perpetrators were sentenced to hang, but the sentences were commuted. Peiper was imprisoned for eleven years for his role in the murders.[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker2012278-253)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Bodies_of_U.S._officers_and_soldiers_slain_by_the_Nazis_after_capture_near_Malmedy%2C_Belgium._-_NARA_-_196544.jpg/250px-Bodies_of_U.S._officers_and_soldiers_slain_by_the_Nazis_after_capture_near_Malmedy%2C_Belgium._-_NARA_-_196544.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bodies_of_U.S._officers_and_soldiers_slain_by_the_Nazis_after_capture_near_Malmedy,_Belgium._-_NARA_-_196544.jpg)

American POWs murdered by SS forces led by [Joachim Peiper](/wiki/Joachim_Peiper "Joachim Peiper") in the [Malmedy massacre](/wiki/Malmedy_massacre "Malmedy massacre") during the [Battle of the Bulge](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Bulge "Battle of the Bulge"), December 1944

In the east, the Red Army resumed its offensive on 12 January 1945. German forces were outnumbered twenty to one in aircraft, eleven to one in infantry, and seven to one in tanks on the Eastern Front.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2011168-254) By the end of the month, the Red Army had made bridgeheads across the [Oder](/wiki/Oder "Oder"), the last geographic obstacle before Berlin.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200270-255) The western Allies continued to advance as well, but not as rapidly as the Red Army.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200283-256) The Panzer Corps conducted a successful defensive operation on 17–24 February at the [Hron](/wiki/Hron "Hron") River, stalling the Allied advance towards Vienna.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy2002293-257) The [1st](/wiki/I_SS_Panzer_Corps "I SS Panzer Corps") and [2nd SS Panzer Corps](/wiki/II_SS_Panzer_Corps "II SS Panzer Corps") made their way towards Austria but were slowed by damaged railways.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZiemke1968439-258)

Budapest fell on 13 February.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200282-259) Hitler ordered Dietrich's [6th Panzer Army](/wiki/6th_Panzer_Army "6th Panzer Army") to move into Hungary to protect the [Nagykanizsa](/wiki/Nagykanizsa "Nagykanizsa") oilfields and refineries, which he deemed the most strategically valuable fuel reserves on the Eastern Front.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeaton1971537-260)[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy2002293-257) *Frühlingserwachsen* ([Operation Spring Awakening](/wiki/Operation_Spring_Awakening "Operation Spring Awakening")), the final German offensive in the east, took place in early March. German forces attacked near Lake Balaton, with 6th Panzer Army advancing north towards Budapest and 2nd Panzer Army moving east and south.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy2002294-261) Dietrich's forces at first made good progress, but as they drew near the Danube, the combination of muddy terrain and strong Soviet resistance brought them to a halt.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002238-262) By 16 March, the battle was lost.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZiemke1968450-263) Enraged by the defeat, Hitler ordered the *Waffen-SS* units involved to remove their [cuff titles](/wiki/Cuff_title "Cuff title") as a mark of disgrace. Dietrich refused to carry out the order.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMessenger2001167–168-264)

By this time, on both the Eastern and Western Front, the activities of the SS were becoming clear to the Allies, as the concentration and extermination camps were being overrun.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachsmann2015542–548-265) Allied troops were filled with disbelief and repugnance at the evidence of Nazi brutality in the camps.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFritz200450–55-266)

On 9 April 1945, [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg") fell to the Red Army, and on 13 April Dietrich's SS unit was forced out of Vienna.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002237-267) The [Battle of Berlin](/wiki/Battle_of_Berlin "Battle of Berlin") began at 03:30 on 16 April with a massive artillery barrage.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2011302-268) Within the week, fighting was taking place inside the city. Among the many elements defending Berlin were French, Latvian, and Scandinavian *Waffen-SS* troops.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002246-269)[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013328,_330,_338-270) Hitler, now residing in the *[Führerbunker](/wiki/F%C3%BChrerbunker "Führerbunker")* under the Reich Chancellery, continued to hope that his remaining SS soldiers could rescue the capital. In spite of the hopelessness of the situation, members of the SS patrolling the city continued to shoot or hang soldiers and civilians for what they considered to be acts of cowardice or defeatism.[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoorhouse2012364–365-271) The Berlin garrison surrendered on 2 May, two days after [Hitler committed suicide](/wiki/Death_of_Adolf_Hitler "Death of Adolf Hitler").[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2011302-268) As members of SS expected little mercy from the Red Army, they attempted to move westward to surrender to the western Allies instead.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002248–249-272)

## SS units and branches

Main article: [Units and commands of the Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Units_and_commands_of_the_Schutzstaffel "Units and commands of the Schutzstaffel")

### Reich Security Main Office

Heydrich held the title of *Chef der Sicherheitspolizei und des SD* (Chief of the Security Police and SD) until 27 September 1939, when he became chief of the newly established Reich Security Main Office (RSHA).[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012470-65)[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeadland199222-273) From that point forward, the RSHA was in charge of SS security services. It had under its command the SD, Kripo, and Gestapo, as well as several offices to handle finance, administration, and supply.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012470-65) [Heinrich Müller](/wiki/Heinrich_M%C3%BCller_(Gestapo) "Heinrich Müller (Gestapo)"), who had been chief of operations for the Gestapo, was appointed Gestapo chief at this time.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2010131-274) [Arthur Nebe](/wiki/Arthur_Nebe "Arthur Nebe") was chief of the Kripo, and the two branches of SD were commanded by a series of SS officers, including [Otto Ohlendorf](/wiki/Otto_Ohlendorf "Otto Ohlendorf") and [Walter Schellenberg](/wiki/Walter_Schellenberg "Walter Schellenberg"). The SD was considered an elite branch of the SS, and its members were better educated and typically more ambitious than those within the ranks of the *Allgemeine SS*.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELangerbein200321-48) Members of the SD were specially trained in criminology, intelligence, and counterintelligence. They also gained a reputation for ruthlessness and unwavering commitment to Nazi ideology.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELangerbein200321–22-275)

Heydrich was attacked in Prague on 27 May 1942 by a British-trained team of Czech and Slovak soldiers who had been sent by the [Czechoslovak government-in-exile](/wiki/Czechoslovak_government-in-exile "Czechoslovak government-in-exile") to assassinate him in [Operation Anthropoid](/wiki/Assassination_of_Reinhard_Heydrich "Assassination of Reinhard Heydrich"). He died from his injuries a week later.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöhne2001494–495-276)[[c]](#cite_note-278) Himmler ran the RSHA personally until 30 January 1943, when Heydrich's positions were taken over by Kaltenbrunner.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012661-279)

### *SS-Sonderkommandos*

This section is about the units within the SS. For the Jewish inmates of death camps who were forced to assist in camp operations, see [Sonderkommandos](/wiki/Sonderkommandos "Sonderkommandos").

Beginning in 1938 and throughout World War II, the SS enacted a procedure where offices and units of the SS could form smaller sub-units, known as *SS-Sonderkommandos*, to carry out special tasks, including large-scale murder operations. The use of *SS-Sonderkommandos* was widespread. According to former SS-*[Sturmbannführer](/wiki/Sturmbannf%C3%BChrer "Sturmbannführer")* [Wilhelm Höttl](/wiki/Wilhelm_H%C3%B6ttl "Wilhelm Höttl"), not even the SS leadership knew how many *SS-Sonderkommandos* were constantly being formed, disbanded, and reformed for various tasks, especially on the Eastern Front.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDiner2006123-280)

An *SS-Sonderkommando* unit led by SS-*Sturmbannführer* [Herbert Lange](/wiki/Herbert_Lange "Herbert Lange") murdered 1,201 psychiatric patients at the [Tiegenhof](/wiki/Nowy_Dw%C3%B3r_Gda%C5%84ski "Nowy Dwór Gdański") psychiatric hospital in the [Free City of Danzig](/wiki/Free_City_of_Danzig "Free City of Danzig"),[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaqueurBaumel2001228-281) 1,100 patients in [Owińska](/wiki/Owi%C5%84ska "Owińska"), 2,750 patients at [Kościan](/wiki/Ko%C5%9Bcian "Kościan"), and 1,558 patients at [Działdowo](/wiki/Dzia%C5%82dowo "Działdowo"), as well as hundreds of Poles at [Fort VII](/wiki/Fort_VII "Fort VII"), where the mobile gas van and gassing bunker were developed.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontague2012188–190-282)[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedlander1997138-283) In 1941–42, *SS-Sonderkommando Lange* set up and managed the first extermination camp, at [Chełmno](/wiki/Che%C5%82mno_extermination_camp "Chełmno extermination camp"), where 152,000 Jews were killed using gas vans.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg2007220-284)

After the [Battle of Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad "Battle of Stalingrad") ended in February 1943, Himmler realised that Germany would likely lose the war and ordered the formation of  [*Sonderkommando* 1005](/wiki/Sonderaktion_1005 "Sonderaktion 1005"), a special task force under SS-*[Standartenführer](/wiki/Standartenf%C3%BChrer "Standartenführer")* [Paul Blobel](/wiki/Paul_Blobel "Paul Blobel"). The unit's assignment was to visit mass graves on the Eastern Front to exhume bodies and burn them in an attempt to cover up the genocide. The task remained unfinished at the end of the war, and many mass graves remain unmarked and unexcavated.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2003258–260,_262-285)

The *Eichmann Sonderkommando* was a task force headed by [Adolf Eichmann](/wiki/Adolf_Eichmann "Adolf Eichmann") that arrived in Budapest on 19 March 1944, the same day that [Axis forces invaded Hungary](/wiki/German_invasion_of_Hungary_(1944) "German invasion of Hungary (1944)"). Their task was to take a direct role in the deportation of Hungarian Jews to Auschwitz. The *SS-Sonderkommandos* enlisted the aid of antisemitic elements from the Hungarian gendarmerie and pro-German administrators from within the Hungarian Interior Ministry.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaqueurBaumel2001195-286) Round-ups began on 16 April, and from 14 May, four trains of 3,000 Jews per day left Hungary and travelled to the camp at Auschwitz II-Birkenau, arriving along a newly built spur line that terminated a few hundred metres from the gas chambers.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010408-287)[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECesarani2005168,_172-288) Between 10 and 25 per cent of the people on each train were chosen as forced labourers; the rest were killed within hours of arrival.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010408-287)[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECesarani2005173-289) Under international pressure, the Hungarian government halted deportations on 6 July 1944, by which time over 437,000 of Hungary's 725,000 Jews had been murdered.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010408-287)[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECesarani2005160,_183-290)

### *Einsatzgruppen*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-A0706-0018-030%2C_Ukraine%2C_ermordete_Familie.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-A0706-0018-030%2C_Ukraine%2C_ermordete_Familie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-A0706-0018-030,_Ukraine,_ermordete_Familie.jpg)

SS murders in [Zboriv](/wiki/Zboriv "Zboriv"), Ukraine, 1941; a teenage boy is brought to view his dead family before being shot himself

The *[Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen")* had its origins in the ad hoc *Einsatzkommando* formed by Heydrich following the *[Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss")* in Austria in March 1938.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreim1989436-291) Two units of *Einsatzgruppen* were stationed in the Sudetenland in October 1938. When military action turned out not to be necessary because of the [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement"), the *Einsatzgruppen* were assigned to confiscate government papers and police documents. They secured government buildings, questioned senior civil servants, and arrested as many as 10,000 Czech communists and German citizens.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreim1989436-291)[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012405,_412-292) The *Einsatzgruppen* also followed *Wehrmacht* troops and killed potential partisans.[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg2007161-293) Similar groups were used in 1939 for the [occupation of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Occupation_of_Czechoslovakia_(1938%E2%80%931945) "Occupation of Czechoslovakia (1938–1945)").[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004109-294)

Hitler felt that the planned extermination of the Jews was too difficult and important to be entrusted to the military.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilberg1985102-295) In 1941 the *Einsatzgruppen* were sent into the Soviet Union to begin large-scale genocide of Jews, Romani people, and communists.[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELangerbein200315–16-296) Historian [Raul Hilberg](/wiki/Raul_Hilberg "Raul Hilberg") estimates that between 1941 and 1945 the *Einsatzgruppen* and related agencies murdered more than two million people, including 1.3 million Jews.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2003257-297) The largest mass shooting perpetrated by the *Einsatzgruppen* was at [Babi Yar](/wiki/Babi_Yar "Babi Yar") outside [Kiev](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), where 33,771 Jews were massacred in a single operation on 29–30 September 1941.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004120–123-298) In the [Rumbula massacre](/wiki/Rumbula_massacre "Rumbula massacre") (November–December 1941), 25,000 victims from the [Riga ghetto](/wiki/Riga_Ghetto "Riga Ghetto") were murdered.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2003210–214-299) In another set of mass shootings (December 1941 – January 1942), the *Einsatzgruppe* massacred over 10,000 Jews at [Drobytsky Yar](/wiki/Drobytsky_Yar "Drobytsky Yar") in [Kharkov](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv").[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZentnerBedürftig1991228-300)

The last *Einsatzgruppen* were disbanded in mid-1944 (although some continued to exist on paper until 1945) due to the German retreat on both fronts and the consequent inability to continue extermination activities. Former *Einsatzgruppen* members were either assigned duties in the *Waffen-SS* or concentration camps. Twenty-four *Einsatzgruppen* commanders were tried for war crimes following the war.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2003274-301)

### SS Court Main Office

The [SS Court Main Office](/wiki/SS_Court_Main_Office "SS Court Main Office") (*Hauptamt SS-Gericht*) was an internal legal system for conducting investigations, trials, and punishment of the SS and police. It had more than 600 lawyers on staff in the main offices in Berlin and Munich. Proceedings were conducted at 38 regional SS courts throughout Germany. It was the only authority authorised to try SS personnel, except for SS members who were on active duty in the *Wehrmacht* (in such cases, the SS member in question was tried by a standard military tribunal). Its creation placed the SS beyond the reach of civilian legal authority. Himmler personally intervened as he saw fit regarding convictions and punishment.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab200937,_40,_41-302) Historian [Karl Dietrich Bracher](/wiki/Karl_Dietrich_Bracher "Karl Dietrich Bracher") describes this court system as one factor in the creation of the Nazi totalitarian police state, as it removed objective legal procedures, rendering citizens defenceless against the "summary justice of the SS terror."[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBracher1970214-303)

### SS Cavalry

Shortly after Hitler seized power in 1933, most horse riding associations were taken over by the SA and SS.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrügerWedemeyer-Kolwe200934-304) Members received combat training to serve in the *Reiter-SS* (SS Cavalry Corps).[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrügerWedemeyer-Kolwe200935-305) The first SS cavalry regiment, designated *SS-Totenkopf Reitstandarte 1*, was formed in September 1939. Commanded by then SS-*Standartenführer* [Hermann Fegelein](/wiki/Hermann_Fegelein "Hermann Fegelein"), the unit was assigned to Poland, where they took part in the extermination of Polish intelligentsia.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013224–225-306)[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPieper201538-307) Additional squadrons were added in May 1940, for a total of fourteen.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013225-308)

The unit was split into two regiments in December 1939, with Fegelein in charge of both. By March 1941 their strength was 3,500 men.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006308-309)[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPieper201552–53-310) In July 1941, they were assigned to the [Pripyat Marshes massacres](/wiki/Pripyat_Marshes_massacres "Pripyat Marshes massacres"), tasked with rounding up and exterminating Jews and partisans in the [Pripyat swamps](/wiki/Pripet_Marshes "Pripet Marshes").[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPieper201581–90-311) The two regiments were amalgamated into the [SS Cavalry Brigade](/wiki/SS_Cavalry_Brigade "SS Cavalry Brigade") on 31 July, twelve days after the operation started.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPieper201581–82-312) Fegelein's final report, dated 18 September 1941, states that they killed 14,178 Jews, 1,001 partisans, and 699 Red Army soldiers, with 830 prisoners taken.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPieper2015119–120-313)[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006310-314) Historian Henning Pieper estimates the actual number of Jews killed was closer to 23,700.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPieper2015120-315) The SS Cavalry Brigade took serious losses in November 1941 in the [Battle of Moscow](/wiki/Battle_of_Moscow "Battle of Moscow"), with casualties of up to 60 per cent in some squadrons.[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPieper2015146–147-316) Fegelein was appointed as commander of the [8th SS Cavalry Division *"Florian Geyer"*](/wiki/8th_SS_Cavalry_Division_Florian_Geyer "8th SS Cavalry Division Florian Geyer") on 20 April 1943. This unit saw service in the Soviet Union in attacks on partisans and civilians.[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013182-317)[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStockert1997229-318) In addition, SS Cavalry regiments served in Croatia and Hungary.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013225–230-319)

### SS Medical Corps

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Selection_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau%2C_1944_%28Auschwitz_Album%29_1b.jpg/250px-Selection_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau%2C_1944_%28Auschwitz_Album%29_1b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Selection_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau,_1944_(Auschwitz_Album)_1b.jpg)

[Hungarian Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Hungary "History of the Jews in Hungary") on the *Judenrampe* (Jewish ramp) after disembarking from the [transport trains](/wiki/Holocaust_trains "Holocaust trains"). Photo from the [Auschwitz Album](/wiki/Auschwitz_Album "Auschwitz Album"), May 1944

Main article: [SS Medical Corps](/wiki/SS_Medical_Corps "SS Medical Corps")

The SS Medical Corps were initially known as the *Sanitätsstaffel* (sanitary units). After 1931, the SS formed the headquarters office *Amt* V as the central office for SS medical units. An SS medical academy was established in Berlin in 1938 to train *Waffen-SS* physicians.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEProctor198886-320) SS medical personnel did not often provide actual medical care; their primary responsibility was medicalised genocide.[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELifton1986147-321) At Auschwitz, about three quarters of new arrivals, including almost all children, women with small children, all the elderly, and all those who appeared on brief and superficial inspection by an SS doctor not to be completely fit were killed within hours of arrival.[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy2006235–237-322) In their role as *Desinfektoren* (disinfectors), SS doctors also made selections among existing prisoners as to their fitness to work and supervised the murder of those deemed unfit. Inmates in deteriorating health were examined by SS doctors, who decided whether or not they would be able to recover in less than two weeks. Those too ill or injured to recover in that time frame were killed.[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELifton1986148–149-323)

At Auschwitz, the actual delivery of gas to the victims was always handled by the SS, on the order of the supervising SS doctor.[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1994170-324)[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiftonHackett1994304-325) Many of the SS doctors also conducted inhumane medical experiments on camp prisoners.[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYahil1990368-326) The most well-known SS doctor, [Josef Mengele](/wiki/Josef_Mengele "Josef Mengele"), served as a medical officer at Auschwitz under the command of [Eduard Wirths](/wiki/Eduard_Wirths "Eduard Wirths") of the camp's medical corps.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYahil1990369-327) Mengele undertook selections even when he was not assigned to do so in the hope of finding subjects for his experiments.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy2006248–249-328) He was particularly interested in locating sets of twins.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPosnerWare198629-329) In contrast to most of the doctors, who viewed undertaking selections as one of their most stressful and horrible duties, Mengele undertook the task with a flamboyant air, often smiling or whistling a tune.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPosnerWare198627-330)[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELifton1985-331) After the war, many SS doctors were charged with war crimes for their medical experiments and for their role in gas chamber selections.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPringle2006294–296-332)

### Other SS units

#### *Ahnenerbe*

The *[Ahnenerbe](/wiki/Ahnenerbe "Ahnenerbe")* (Ancestral Heritage Organisation) was founded in 1935 by Himmler and became part of the SS in 1939.[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpielvogel1992108-333) It was an umbrella agency for more than fifty organisations tasked with studying German racial identity and ancient Germanic traditions and language.[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpielvogel1992108-333)[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne2010132–133-334) The agency sponsored archaeological expeditions in Germany, Scandinavia, the Middle East, Tibet, and elsewhere to search for evidence of Aryan roots, influence, and superiority.[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne2010128–131,_139,_142-335) Further planned expeditions were postponed indefinitely at the start of the war.[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne2010141-336)

#### *SS-Helferinnenkorps*

The *[SS-Helferinnenkorps](/wiki/SS-Helferinnenkorps "SS-Helferinnenkorps")* (lit. 'SS Women’s Auxiliary Corps') were female auxiliary members of the *Waffen-SS*.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOepenRosenWunsch2020339-337) The organization was created to free men for combat by assigning women to non-combat support roles such as administration, communications, and logistics.[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilesCross2008163–164-338) Established in 1942 under the direction of [Ernst Sachs](/wiki/Ernst_Sachs_(SS-Obergruppenf%C3%BChrer) "Ernst Sachs (SS-Obergruppenführer)"), it was Himmler's intention to create a "sister organisation to the Schutzstaffel".[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMühlenberg2011b38–44-339) Around 10,000 women served in the *SS-Helferinnenkorps*, in addition to 15,000 police auxiliaries. They were present in diverse areas, from the offices of the [Reich Security Main Office](/wiki/Reich_Security_Main_Office "Reich Security Main Office") in Berlin to the [concentration camps](/wiki/Concentration_camp "Concentration camp").[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarz1997182-340)

In 1942, Himmler set up the *Reichsschule für SS Helferinnen* (Reich School for SS Helpers) in [Oberehnheim](/wiki/Obernai "Obernai") to train women in communications, again to free up men for combat roles. Himmler intended to replace all female civilian employees in his service with *SS-Helferinnen* members, as they were selected and trained according to Nazi ideology.[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECentury2011-341)[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERempel1989223–224-342) The school was closed on 22 November 1944 due to the Allied advance.[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMühlenberg2011a27-343)

#### *SS-Gefolge*

Main article: [Female guards in Nazi concentration camps](/wiki/Female_guards_in_Nazi_concentration_camps "Female guards in Nazi concentration camps")

The *SS-Gefolge* (lit. 'SS entourage') served as civilian employees without formal SS membership or combat training. They were affiliated with the *Waffen-SS*. Their roles were primarily administrative, working as guards and auxiliaries in concentration camps, with 3,517 female guards (comprising 10% of the total in January 1945), including around 200 at Auschwitz. Often trained at [Ravensbrück](/wiki/Ravensbr%C3%BCck_concentration_camp "Ravensbrück concentration camp"), they took loyalty oaths and enforced camp policies tied to [The Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"), [sterilization](/wiki/Nazi_human_experimentation#Sterilization_and_fertility_experiments "Nazi human experimentation"), and [euthanasia](/wiki/Aktion_T4 "Aktion T4"), and supervised prisoners, aiding [Nazi racial policies](/wiki/Racial_policy_of_Nazi_Germany "Racial policy of Nazi Germany").[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERuda2023389-344)[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes1991302-345)

## Foreign legions and volunteers

See also: [Waffen-SS foreign volunteers and conscripts](/wiki/Waffen-SS_foreign_volunteers_and_conscripts "Waffen-SS foreign volunteers and conscripts")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1980-036-05%2C_Amin_al_Husseini_bei_bosnischen_SS-Freiwilligen.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1980-036-05%2C_Amin_al_Husseini_bei_bosnischen_SS-Freiwilligen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1980-036-05,_Amin_al_Husseini_bei_bosnischen_SS-Freiwilligen.jpg)

[Grand Mufti of Jerusalem](/wiki/Grand_Mufti_of_Jerusalem "Grand Mufti of Jerusalem") [Amin al-Husseini](/wiki/Amin_al-Husseini "Amin al-Husseini") greeting Bosnian *Waffen-SS* volunteers before their departure to the Eastern Front, 1943

Beginning in 1940, Himmler opened up *Waffen-SS* recruiting to ethnic Germans that were not German citizens.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty2004160-346) In March 1941, the SS Main Office established the *Germanische Leitstelle* (Germanic Guidance Office) to establish *Waffen-SS* recruiting offices in Nazi-occupied Europe.[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoehl2004212–213-347) The majority of the resulting foreign *Waffen-SS* units wore a distinctive national collar patch and preceded their SS rank titles with the prefix *Waffen* instead of SS. Volunteers from Scandinavian countries filled the ranks of two divisions, the *SS-Wiking* and *[SS-Nordland](/wiki/11th_SS_Volunteer_Panzergrenadier_Division_Nordland "11th SS Volunteer Panzergrenadier Division Nordland")*.[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoehl2004214–219-348) Swiss German speakers joined in substantial numbers.[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutmann2017Chapter_3-349) Belgian Flemings joined Dutchmen to form the *[SS-Nederland](/wiki/Volunteer_Legion_Netherlands "Volunteer Legion Netherlands")* legion,[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013272–273-350) and their Walloon compatriots joined the *[SS-Wallonien](/wiki/Walloon_Legion "Walloon Legion")*.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013321–323-351) By the end of 1943 about a quarter of the SS were ethnic Germans from across Europe,[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöhne2001458-352) and by June 1944, half the *Waffen-SS* were foreign nationals.[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012306-353)

Additional *Waffen-SS* units were added from the [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainian_collaboration_with_Nazi_Germany "Ukrainian collaboration with Nazi Germany"), [Albanians](/wiki/21st_Waffen_Mountain_Division_of_the_SS_Skanderbeg "21st Waffen Mountain Division of the SS Skanderbeg") from [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), Serbians, Croatians, Turkic, Caucasians, Cossack, and Tatars. The Ukrainians and Tatars, who had suffered persecution under [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), were likely motivated primarily by opposition to the Soviet government rather than ideological agreement with the SS.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReitlinger1989200–204-354) The exiled Grand Mufti of Jerusalem [Amin al-Husseini](/wiki/Amin_al-Husseini "Amin al-Husseini") was made an SS-*Gruppenführer* by Himmler in May 1943.[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReitlinger1989199-355) He subsequently used antisemitism and anti-Serb racism to recruit a *Waffen-SS* division of [Bosnian Muslims](/wiki/Bosniaks "Bosniaks"), the *[SS-Handschar](/wiki/13th_Waffen_Mountain_Division_of_the_SS_Handschar_(1st_Croatian) "13th Waffen Mountain Division of the SS Handschar (1st Croatian)")*.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHale2011264–266-356) The year-long Soviet [occupation of the Baltic states](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Baltic_states "Occupation of the Baltic states") at the beginning of World War II resulted in volunteers for [Latvian](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") and [Estonian](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") *Waffen-SS* units. The [Estonian Legion](/wiki/Estonian_Legion "Estonian Legion") had 1,280 volunteers under training by the end of 1942.[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBishop200593-357) Approximately 25,000 men served in the Estonian SS division, with thousands more conscripted into Police Front battalions and border guard units.[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBishop200593–94-358) Most of the Estonians were fighting primarily to regain their independence and as many as 15,000 of them died fighting alongside the Germans.[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMüller2012169-359) In early 1944, Himmler even contacted Pohl to suggest releasing Muslim prisoners from concentration camps to supplement his SS troops.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMotadel2014242-360)

The [Indian Legion](/wiki/Indian_Legion "Indian Legion") was a *Wehrmacht* unit formed in August 1942 chiefly from disaffected Indian soldiers of the [British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army") captured in the [North African Campaign](/wiki/North_African_campaign "North African campaign"). In August 1944 it was transferred to the auspices of the *Waffen-SS* as the *Indische Freiwilligen-Legion der Waffen-SS*.[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2002189-361) There was also a French volunteer division, *[SS-Charlemagne](/wiki/33rd_Waffen_Grenadier_Division_of_the_SS_Charlemagne "33rd Waffen Grenadier Division of the SS Charlemagne")*, which was formed in 1944 mainly from the remnants of the [Legion of French Volunteers Against Bolshevism](/wiki/Legion_of_French_Volunteers_Against_Bolshevism "Legion of French Volunteers Against Bolshevism") and French *Sturmbrigade*.[[359]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab2013326–330-362)

## Ranks and uniforms

Main article: [Uniforms and insignia of the Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Uniforms_and_insignia_of_the_Schutzstaffel "Uniforms and insignia of the Schutzstaffel")

See also: [Runic insignia of the Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Runic_insignia_of_the_Schutzstaffel "Runic insignia of the Schutzstaffel")

The SS established its own symbolism, rituals, customs, ranks, and uniforms to set itself apart from other organisations. Before 1929, the SS wore the same brown uniform as the SA, with the addition of a black tie and a black cap with a *[Totenkopf](/wiki/Totenkopf "Totenkopf")* (death's head) skull and bones symbol, moving to an all-black uniform in 1932.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaqueurBaumel2001604-14)[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNab201390-363) In 1935, the SS combat formations adopted a service uniform in field grey for everyday wear. The SS also developed its own field uniforms, which included reversible smocks and helmet covers printed with [camouflage](/wiki/Camouflage "Camouflage") patterns.[[361]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty200488–92-364) Uniforms were manufactured in hundreds of licensed factories, with some workers being prisoners of war performing forced labour. Many were produced in concentration camps.[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGivhan1997-365)

Hitler and the Nazi Party understood the power of emblems and insignia to influence public opinion.[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne201064-366) The stylised lightning bolt logo of the SS was chosen in 1932. The logo is a pair of runes from a set of 18 [Armanen runes](/wiki/Armanen_runes "Armanen runes") created by [Guido von List](/wiki/Guido_von_List "Guido von List") in 1906. It is similar to the ancient [Sowilō](/wiki/Sowil%C5%8D_(rune) "Sowilō (rune)") rune, which symbolises the sun, but was renamed as "Sig" (victory) in List's iconography.[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne201064-366) The *Totenkopf* symbolised the wearer's willingness to fight unto the death, and also served to frighten the enemy.[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYenne201069-367)

## SS membership estimates 1925–1945

After 1933 a career in the SS became increasingly attractive to Germany's social elite, who began joining the movement in great numbers, usually motivated by political opportunism. By 1938 about one-third of the SS leadership were members of the [upper middle class](/wiki/Upper_middle_class "Upper middle class"). The trend reversed after the first Soviet counter-offensive of 1942.[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZiegler2014132–134_and_note_13-368)

    | 1926 | 200[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201232-370) | [Joseph Berchtold](/wiki/Joseph_Berchtold "Joseph Berchtold")[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale201230-371) |

## SS offices

By 1942 all activities of the SS were managed through twelve main offices.[[377]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYerger199713–21-380)[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg2007302-381)

* [Personal Staff *Reichsführer-SS*](/wiki/Personal_Staff_Reichsf%C3%BChrer-SS "Personal Staff Reichsführer-SS")
  + *[Kommandostab Reichsführer-SS](/wiki/Kommandostab_Reichsf%C3%BChrer-SS "Kommandostab Reichsführer-SS")* (Command Staff *Reichsführer-SS*)
* [SS Main Office](/wiki/SS_Main_Office "SS Main Office") (SS-HA)
* *[SS-Führungshauptamt](/wiki/SS_F%C3%BChrungshauptamt "SS Führungshauptamt")* (SS Main Operational Office; SS-FHA)
* [Reich Security Main Office](/wiki/Reich_Security_Main_Office "Reich Security Main Office") (RSHA)
* [SS Main Economic and Administrative Office](/wiki/SS_Main_Economic_and_Administrative_Office "SS Main Economic and Administrative Office") (WVHA)
* *[Ordnungspolizei Hauptamt](/wiki/Ordnungspolizei#Organization "Ordnungspolizei")* (Main Office of the Order Police)
* [SS Court Main Office](/wiki/SS_Court_Main_Office "SS Court Main Office")

## Austrian SS

Main article: [Austrian SS](/wiki/Austrian_SS "Austrian SS")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-45534-0005%2C_Kz_Mauthausen%2C_Besuch_Heinrich_Himmler%2C_Franz_Ziereis.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-45534-0005%2C_Kz_Mauthausen%2C_Besuch_Heinrich_Himmler%2C_Franz_Ziereis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-45534-0005,_Kz_Mauthausen,_Besuch_Heinrich_Himmler,_Franz_Ziereis.jpg)

[Ernst Kaltenbrunner](/wiki/Ernst_Kaltenbrunner "Ernst Kaltenbrunner"), Himmler, [August Eigruber](/wiki/August_Eigruber "August Eigruber"), and other SS officials visiting Mauthausen concentration camp, 1941

The term "Austrian SS" is often used to describe that portion of the SS membership from Austria, but it was never a recognised branch of the SS. In contrast to SS members from other countries, who were grouped into either the Germanic-SS or the Foreign Legions of the *Waffen-SS*, Austrian SS members were regular SS personnel. It was technically under the command of the SS in Germany but often acted independently concerning Austrian affairs. The Austrian SS was founded in 1930 and by 1934 was acting as a covert force to bring about the *Anschluss* with Germany, which occurred in March 1938. Early Austrian SS leaders were Kaltenbrunner and [Arthur Seyss-Inquart](/wiki/Arthur_Seyss-Inquart "Arthur Seyss-Inquart").[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowder1996205–206-382) Austrian SS members served in every branch of the SS. Austrians constituted 8 per cent of Nazi Germany's population and 13 per cent of the SS; 40 per cent of the staff and 75 per cent of commanders at death camps were Austrian.[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArt200643-383)

After the *Anschluss*, the Austrian SS was folded into *[SS-Oberabschnitt Donau](/wiki/Austrian_SS "Austrian SS")*. The third regiment of the *SS-Verfügungstruppe* (*Der Führer*) and the fourth *Totenkopf* regiment (*Ostmark*) were recruited in Austria shortly thereafter. On Heydrich's orders, mass arrests of potential enemies of the Reich began immediately after the *Anschluss*.[[381]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerwarth2011120–121-384) [Mauthausen](/wiki/Mauthausen_concentration_camp "Mauthausen concentration camp") was the first concentration camp opened in Austria following the *Anschluss*.[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012107-385) Before the invasion of the Soviet Union, Mauthausen was the harshest of the camps in the Greater German Reich.[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerwarth2011121-386)

The [Hotel Metropole](/wiki/Hotel_Metropole,_Vienna "Hotel Metropole, Vienna") was transformed into the headquarters for the Gestapo in Vienna in April 1938. With a staff of 900 (80 per cent of whom were recruited from the Austrian police), it was the largest Gestapo office outside Berlin. An estimated 50,000 people were interrogated or tortured there.[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson2011-387) The Gestapo in Vienna was headed by [Franz Josef Huber](/wiki/Franz_Josef_Huber "Franz Josef Huber"), who also served as chief of the [Central Agency for Jewish Emigration in Vienna](/wiki/Central_Agency_for_Jewish_Emigration_in_Vienna "Central Agency for Jewish Emigration in Vienna"). Although its de facto leaders were Adolf Eichmann and later [Alois Brunner](/wiki/Alois_Brunner "Alois Brunner"), Huber was nevertheless responsible for the mass deportation of Austrian Jews.[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMang20031–5-388)

## Post-war activity and aftermath

Following Nazi Germany's collapse, the SS ceased to exist.[[386]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöhne2001580-389) Numerous members of the SS, many of them still committed Nazis, remained at large in Germany and across Europe.[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008739–741-390) On 21 May 1945, the British captured Himmler, who was in disguise and carrying a fraudulent passport. At an internment camp near [Lüneburg](/wiki/L%C3%BCneburg "Lüneburg"), he committed suicide by biting down on a cyanide capsule.[[388]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012736-391) Several other leading members of the SS fled, but some were quickly captured. [Ernst Kaltenbrunner](/wiki/Ernst_Kaltenbrunner "Ernst Kaltenbrunner"), chief of the RSHA and the highest-ranking surviving SS main department chief upon Himmler's suicide, was captured and arrested in the [Bavarian Alps](/wiki/Bavarian_Alps "Bavarian Alps").[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeale2012410-392) He was among the 22 defendants put on trial at the [International Military Tribunal](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials") in 1945–46.[[390]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurleigh2000803–804-393)

Some SS members were subject to [summary execution](/wiki/Summary_execution "Summary execution"), torture, and beatings at the hands of freed prisoners, displaced persons, or Allied soldiers.[[391]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonogh20093-394)[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurrayMillett2001565–568-395) American soldiers of the 157th Regiment, who entered the concentration camp at Dachau in April 1945 and viewed the acts committed by the SS, [shot some of the remaining SS camp guards](/wiki/Dachau_liberation_reprisals "Dachau liberation reprisals").[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELowe201283–84-396) On 15 April 1945, British troops entered Bergen-Belsen. They placed the SS guards on starvation rations, made them work without breaks, forced them to deal with the remaining corpses, and stabbed them with bayonets or struck them with their rifle butts if they slowed their pace.[[394]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELowe201284–87-397) Some members of the [US Army Counter Intelligence Corps](/wiki/Counterintelligence_Corps "Counterintelligence Corps") delivered captured SS camp guards to [displaced person camps](/wiki/Displaced_persons_camps_in_post%E2%80%93World_War_II_Europe "Displaced persons camps in post–World War II Europe"), where they knew they would be subject to summary execution.[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrzezinski2005-398)

### International Military Tribunal at Nuremberg

Main article: [Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Dead_ernstkaltenbrunner.jpg/330px-Dead_ernstkaltenbrunner.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dead_ernstkaltenbrunner.jpg)

The body of Ernst Kaltenbrunner after his execution on 16 October 1946

The Allies commenced legal proceedings against captured Nazis, establishing the International Military Tribunal at Nuremberg in 1945.[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008741-399) The first [war crimes](/wiki/War_crimes "War crimes") trial of 24 prominent figures such as Göring, [Albert Speer](/wiki/Albert_Speer "Albert Speer"), [Joachim von Ribbentrop](/wiki/Joachim_von_Ribbentrop "Joachim von Ribbentrop"), [Alfred Rosenberg](/wiki/Alfred_Rosenberg "Alfred Rosenberg"), Hans Frank, and Kaltenbrunner took place beginning in November 1945. They were accused of four counts: conspiracy, waging a war of aggression, war crimes, and crimes against humanity in violation of international law.[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008741-399) Twelve received the death penalty, including Kaltenbrunner, who was convicted of crimes against humanity and executed on 16 October 1946.[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008741–742-400) The former commandant at Auschwitz, [Rudolf Höss](/wiki/Rudolf_H%C3%B6ss "Rudolf Höss"), who testified on behalf of Kaltenbrunner and others, was tried and executed in 1947.[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008743-401)

Additional SS trials and convictions followed.[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurleigh2000804-402) Many defendants attempted to exculpate themselves using the excuse that they were merely following [superior orders](/wiki/Superior_orders "Superior orders"), which they had to obey unconditionally as part of their [sworn oath](/wiki/Oaths_to_Hitler "Oaths to Hitler") and duty. The courts did not find this to be a legitimate defence.[[400]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngrao2013240–241-403) A trial of 40 SS officers and guards from Auschwitz took place in Kraków in November 1947. Most were found guilty, and 23 received the death penalty.[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008743–744-404) The twelve [subsequent Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Subsequent_Nuremberg_trials "Subsequent Nuremberg trials") took place in 1946–1949; also, an estimated 37,000 members of the SS were tried and convicted in Soviet courts. Sentences included hangings and long terms of hard labour.[[402]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurleigh2010549-405) [Piotr Cywiński](/wiki/Piotr_Cywi%C5%84ski "Piotr Cywiński"), director of the Auschwitz-Birkenau Museum, estimates that of the 70,000 members of the SS involved in crimes in concentration camps, only about 1,650 to 1,700 were tried after the war.[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosackiUhligWróblewski2008-406) The International Military Tribunal declared the SS a criminal organisation in 1946.[[404]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZentnerBedürftig1991906-407)

### Escapes

See also: [Ratlines (World War II aftermath)](/wiki/Ratlines_(World_War_II_aftermath) "Ratlines (World War II aftermath)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/WP_Eichmann_Passport.jpg/250px-WP_Eichmann_Passport.jpg)](/wiki/File:WP_Eichmann_Passport.jpg)

[Red Cross](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Red_Cross "International Committee of the Red Cross") passport under the name of "Ricardo Klement" that [Adolf Eichmann](/wiki/Adolf_Eichmann "Adolf Eichmann") used to enter Argentina in 1950

After the war, many former Nazis fled to South America, especially to Argentina, where they were welcomed by [Juan Perón](/wiki/Juan_Per%C3%B3n "Juan Perón")'s regime.[[405]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy2006143–144-408) In the 1950s, former Dachau inmate Lothar Hermann discovered that [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires") resident Ricardo Klement was, in fact, Adolf Eichmann, who had in 1948 obtained false identification and a landing permit for Argentina through an organisation directed by Bishop [Alois Hudal](/wiki/Alois_Hudal "Alois Hudal"), an Austrian cleric with Nazi sympathies, then living in Italy.[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECesarani2005207-409) Eichmann was captured in Buenos Aires on 11 May 1960 by [Mossad](/wiki/Mossad "Mossad"), the Israeli intelligence agency. At his trial in Jerusalem in 1961, he was found guilty and sentenced to death by hanging. Eichmann was quoted as having stated, "I will jump into my grave laughing because the fact that I have the death of five million Jews [or Reich enemies, as he later claimed to have said] on my conscience gives me extraordinary satisfaction."[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArendt200646-410) [Franz Stangl](/wiki/Franz_Stangl "Franz Stangl"), the commandant of Treblinka, also escaped to South America with the assistance of Hudal's network. He was deported to Germany in 1967 and was sentenced to life in prison in 1970. He died in 1971.[[408]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008746–747-411)

Mengele, worried that his capture would mean a death sentence, fled Germany on 17 April 1949.[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy2006263-412) Assisted by a network of former SS members, he travelled to [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), where he obtained a passport under the alias "Helmut Gregor" from the [International Committee of the Red Cross](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Red_Cross "International Committee of the Red Cross"). He sailed to Argentina in July.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy2006264–265-413) Aware that he was still a wanted man, he moved to Paraguay in 1958 and Brazil in 1960. In both instances he was assisted by former *[Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe")* pilot [Hans-Ulrich Rudel](/wiki/Hans-Ulrich_Rudel "Hans-Ulrich Rudel").[[411]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy2006269,_273-414) Mengele suffered a stroke while swimming and drowned in 1979.[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy2006294–295-415)

Thousands of Nazis, including former SS members such as Trawniki guard [Jakob Reimer](/wiki/Jakob_Reimer "Jakob Reimer") and Circassian collaborator [Tscherim Soobzokov](/wiki/Tscherim_Soobzokov "Tscherim Soobzokov"), fled to the United States under the guise of refugees, sometimes using forged documents.[[413]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELichtblau20142–3,_10–11-416) Other SS men, such as Soobzokov, SD officer [Wilhelm Höttl](/wiki/Wilhelm_H%C3%B6ttl "Wilhelm Höttl"), Eichmann aide [Otto von Bolschwing](/wiki/Otto_von_Bolschwing "Otto von Bolschwing"), and accused war criminal [Theodor Saevecke](/wiki/Theo_Saevecke "Theo Saevecke"), were employed by American intelligence agencies against the Soviets. As [CIA](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") officer Harry Rositzke noted, "It was a visceral business of using any bastard so long as he was anti-Communist. ... The eagerness or desire to enlist collaborators means that sure, you didn't look at their credentials too closely."[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELichtblau201429–30,_32–37,_67–68-417) Similarly, the Soviets used SS personnel after the war; Operation Theo, for instance, disseminated "subversive rumours" in Allied-occupied Germany.[[415]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiddiscombe2000131–143-418)

[Simon Wiesenthal](/wiki/Simon_Wiesenthal "Simon Wiesenthal") and others have speculated about the existence of a Nazi fugitive network code-named [ODESSA](/wiki/ODESSA "ODESSA") (an acronym for *Organisation der ehemaligen SS-Angehörigen*, Organisation of former SS members) that allegedly helped war criminals find refuge in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESegev2010106–108-419) British writer [Gitta Sereny](/wiki/Gitta_Sereny "Gitta Sereny"), who conducted interviews with SS men, considers the story untrue and attributes the escapes to postwar chaos and Hudal's Vatican-based network. While the existence of ODESSA remains unproven, Sereny notes that "there certainly were various kinds of Nazi aid organisations after the war—it would have been astonishing if there hadn't been."[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESereny1974274-420)
